feedback: All the instructions were clear. I would suggest maybe increasing the pay for the time required for completion.
machiv_clean_interpersonal_tactics_5_spe: If I had already discuss this matter with my supervisor, and they indicated they preferred harmony over honest feedback, I think I would be lining up a new job. I find it anathema to withhold criticism to maintain harmony, especially if this is feedback critical to business success. If I were reliant on this pay check to stay alive, I would probably bite my tongue for the time being while doing an aggressive job search. I would leave as soon as possible. It is untenable and unprofitable. If my expertise isn't trusted, it makes for a miserable environment. I would feel disrespected. Probably my work would suffer.
machiv_clean_views_of_human_nature_13_br: I think this is a complex question with no definitive answer. It can be situational.  I think most human will act immediately to help a child in a dangerous situation. However, the same individual who puts their life own the line to extract a child from a burning building or flood, may ignore unethical practices at their place of work. Sometimes this could be inertia or it could be their livelihood is threatened if they act as a whistleblower. Sometimes something like bystander effect comes into play. There is an expectation others will go first and be brave. I think environment plays a role. I think the pendulum swings back and forth in regards to courage. Currently, there is not a lot of courage in our institutions. We may be at our nadir at a societal level. There are still brave individuals working to uphold the constitutional order and rule of law, but I would say they are currently in the minority. I have to believe that there are heroes in waiting. The pendulum will swing back.
npi_clean_factor0_na_9_broad: Because I am very selective about my friends and who I choose to share information with, I would say in general people listen attentively to what I share. Whether or not they enjoy it, depends or what the topic is. I expect honesty from people I am close to, so it is clear when they are entertained versus when they are listening strictly because they know the subject is important to me. If my friends are tired of a subject, they will inform me. If they are amused it is clear. I think they are enjoying the conversation, they will actively participate by sharing their experiences or opinion on a topic.
npi_clean_factor1_grandioseexhibitionism: I don't really receive compliments like this. I prefer constructive criticism to any sort of effusive praise. I have very high standards so in general I don't find praise to represent the objective quality of work I do. I think everything can be improved. If I receive a compliment, I learned long ago that it is poor form to disagree and point out why the individual is wrong. I just say thank you-especially if I am not particularly close to the person. People who understand my perspective know to always give balanced, constructive feedback. Praise alone is just as useless as hyper-criticism.
srp_clean_factor1_interpersonalmanipulat: I don't like to misrepresent myself in any fashion. I don't exaggerate my experience, my skill set, or shape my personality to be more appealing. I find the latter especially distasteful. I would approach the situation with honesty. If I am passionate about the collaboration and think I can make a contribution, I would state why without lying about my background. If I miss out on the opportunity because my skills are not a good match, I would work on building up those skills so next time I am fully prepared to take on the role. I would see this as an opportunity to grow whether or not I get to collaborate.
srp_clean_factor2_criminaltendencies_13_: I have never used threats or pressure to get something I wanted. I inform a person of the situation and explain how important a result is to my well-being-whether it is financial or psychological-and leave it at that. Either they help me or they don't. I find it repugnant to bully a person into doing what I want. I have always found being honest about my needs is highly effective. It once again may come down to choosing quality human beings to associate with. When people tell me no, there is always a good reason. Every person I associate with roots for my success.

feedback: The instructions were clear. The question were clear.
machiv_clean_interpersonal_tactics_4_bro: I think there's no reason to answer questions when people ask you them. Complete honesty implies that you tell everyone everything that they ask. But you aren't required to tell anyone anything about yourself. You can live a private life. I think that people who invade other people's privacy should pause. You should live your own life and not concern yourself with me. Being honest and not lying is important. So I tell the truth. But I don't answer questions I don't choose to. Some people should just mind their own business. And then the world will be a better place for a lot of people. Because forcing others into the things that you want to do is wrong. So if you ask me a question and you don't get an answer just assume that I'm not going to lie to you.
machiv_clean_views_of_human_nature_13_sp: It depends. Sometimes when you see people being picked on it's not genuine. More and more the world is being staged. There's no reason to step in in a stage scene. Some people just like attention. Some people like to try and create situations for other people to be uncomfortable in. And it just might be you watching this that is the one who is uncomfortable. You have to pick and choose. Sometimes you say something. Sometimes you don't. Why would you say something if that's the reaction that people were trying to get out of you. You have to be cognizant of the fact that some people might just be trying to get a rise of you specifically.
npi_clean_factor0_na_8_specific: I am different. I have special things about my personality. I'm not typical. Things about me stand out. I don't try to stand out they just do. My personality doesn't necessarily glow. I try not to stand out. I prefer to just blend in. And I generally try to avoid crowds because I don't really want to be around people anyways. So I don't think I'm special. Because those people are egotistical and self-centered. Everybody deserves for things to be good in their life. I just look for things to be fair. I expect everyone to be at their best in terms of their behavior. And that's just generally the world.
npi_clean_factor2_leadershipauthority_4_: I wonder what makes people think that other people should give them directions. Children have to follow directions. Adults should follow the laws of the universe. You cannot walk all over other people. You should leave other people alone. But other people aren't expected to follow someone else's directions. I don't even necessarily agree with work Arrangements where someone gets to tell me what to do. So generally I choose what I'm going to do. And I do what is correct. So no one gets to tell me what to do. And I don't necessarily want to tell other people what to do. Every adult should be able to figure out what needs to be done. And that's how life should work.
srp_clean_factor1_callousaffect_5_specif: I don't like violent anything. I try to avoid violence. And I don't understand violent sports. I don't think the two should cross. Sports should be for fun. Someone being hurt that person is not having fun. I don't find it fun to watch a person be hurt. I don't even like sports where a person could be hurt. I don't like sports in general but I can watch baseball I can watch basketball. Football concerns me. Boxing I avoid. We should all play fair and be nice to people who are nice and as nice as we can be in each situation whatever that situation is as nice as you can be. But sometimes you have to be mean. You don't always have to be violent. But if a person approaches me with violence. That might require me becoming violent.
srp_clean_factor2_criminaltendencies_13_: What will threats do. What will pressure do. People pay for the things they do wrong. People generally Live Well if they do the right thing. I try to do the right thing. Sometimes things don't go the way I want them to. Deal with things as they are until things can be better for me. I try to make a better way for myself. But that doesn't mean that it has anything to do with any other people. And if other people are the problem. I anticipate that the universe will take care of those people. You don't get to become a problem for other people just because you choose to be. The world is enough space and enough resources for everyone. So if someone is in my way. I know that the wave will be cleared.

feedback: No questions were confusing. All was clear and straightforward. I enjoyed writing. The questions were unexpected but interesting.
machiv_clean_morality_7_specific: I would acknowledge both successes and setbacks because I'm not one to be boastful in front of others. I don't brag. If I choose to tell someone of an accomplishment, I do so very cautiously. I myself despise arrogant braggarts who continually see life as a competition with others and have to tell all of their accomplishments and look down on others. The reality is, no one has a perfect life with no mistakes or regrets. Being truthful and honest and transparent has great advantage in that people are more inclined to listen to you and relate to you in a down-to-earth way. They're not feeling like they need to be on the defense all the time. They know they have faults and also know your own, so why brag like you don't have any faults or failures? People see through it.
machiv_clean_views_of_human_nature_9_bro: I believe most people who work hard are self-motivated because this inner drive helps them to simply get something they want. They work hard to get money. They work hard to please people in their workspace. They work hard to get approval from friends and family. They work hard because of their own pride and arrogance, concerned with their reputation. All of these reasons are for their own desires. Even if someone might be forced to work hard from outside influences, such as deadlines, the only reason they keep going is for the self. That’s why they don’t give up. They have to have their own goals met in order to continue.
npi_clean_factor0_na_8_specific: I don't mind being treated generally as an average person, especially as I grow older. I'm not better than anyone else I think, out of foolish pride, many young people think they're extra special compared to everyone else, but, as you grow older, you realize you make mistakes and have a lot of faults. I now don't think of myself as special compared to others like I used to. If the truth be known, each and every person on this earth is special and unique. Just look at our DNA. No one is the same. Every fingerprint is different. We all have talents and gifts of our own. I believe others pick up on it if you think you're better than others or more special and need others to make you feel that way. People don't like that. I know I don't, so I don't want to be that person who thinks he/she is special and needs to be treated as such. It's very unattractive.
npi_clean_factor3_entitlementexploitativ: It's always appealing when coming across someone who is a constant giver and is not demanding. In dealing with a person who is always unhappy because he/she isn't getting what they want, it's very tiresome. They're not getting what they think they deserve, so they are always unhappy. I don't want to be like this, so I try not to let my mind go down that path of selfish expectancy from others. I try to focus on what I can give because this strengthens relationships and shows love. Real love means you don't expect anything in return, though no one's perfect at this all the time.
srp_clean_factor1_callousaffect_6_specif: Yes, I would try to reconnect in a very cautious way. I'd think back to that person and what I recall and how I felt at the time of this past friendship. I don't generally trust people, so this is normal for me. The way I would decide is based on the demeanor and words of this childhood friend. People change over time because of experiences, and this person may not be the same anymore. That's why I'm cautious. Also, I know I've changed in my values and priorities, so I will use discernment when deciding whether or not to help this person. I'm not focused, though, on what I'm getting or not getting out of the relationship in this case. I simply hesitate naturally when someone is wanting something from me, and I may not know what it is. If it's something really simple and fast, I'd offer help, but I'm not making any long-term commitments to be great friends again. In my own life, friendships do fade and shift, and it's often for a good reason, making me a better person than I was before because of what I've learned from that relationship.
srp_clean_factor2_criminaltendencies_12_: Yes, I think about my personal safety constantly. I own a pistol for my protection. At times, when we lived in a different area and I was in my fifties, I walked almost every day for exercise around parks, cemeteries, neighborhoods and carried my pistol around my waist in a holster. My spouse worked 45 minutes away, and we didn't know more than a handful of people in this new town. I felt much safer walking by myself as a woman. Otherwise, I do carry pepper spray in my purse and bear spray in our car and feel safer as we drive in large cities, such as Chicago. Though I feel safe in my town, I do not completely trust people and am highly aware of my surroundings everywhere I go. Anything can happen, so I need to be prepared for it. Even here at home, I have protective "clubs" or sticks nearby in case our home is broken into. I decide if I'm going to carry a weapon based on whether I'm alone or not and the environment. Big city, yes. Alone on a walk, yes. With family or friends, no.

feedback: I enjoyed the assignment
machiv_clean_interpersonal_tactics_3_spe: It depends how much flattery is being given. I think to an extent it’s warranted, but morally too much would be coming on very quick level wise. I think it’s best to assess how much is at stake. Determine how much of your ego you want to pair with being successful. There’s a fine line where both can work at times. A little bit of both and in the right situations. Also depends of the mental makeup of the person you’re giving the flattery too. A lot of variables at play here in this situation. All need to be toned and corrected from situation to situation.
machiv_clean_views_of_human_nature_13_br: I think more people are risk adversed than people are brave. A lot of people like to avoid conflict more than anything. Part of having courage is feeling uncomfortable. To embrace the unknown. To help a situation or someone and not expect any reward or applause back your way. Bravery that i’ve seen the last few months is my friend giving money to homeless people. He told me what they did with that money afterwards was none of his business as they have a hard enough life as is. My friend who never seeks any fame or attention from his good deeds. I think humbleness is a key part of being brave as well.
npi_clean_factor0_na_10_specific: I consider what I might learn from their perspectives as well. Me earlier on in my career would feel more selfish but I’ve learned to see that colleagues’ plans and ideas have been better than mine sometimes in the past. It’s always worth seeing their side of the equation. Bounce ideas off one another as well is always a nice learning experience. The more ideas the better. I think that’s key for any situation and obstacle in life. To have ideas and resources to be successful. To set yourself in the best way possible to come out ahead. To have everyone on board no matter what to tackle a situation.
npi_clean_factor1_grandioseexhibitionism: I’ll usually talk to one or two people towards the side of the room. I never do like being the center of attention. I’m also a laid back person in general. I like quiet gatherings, parties, etc. Nothing too festive or loud. I like to admire and soak up the surroundings. Take everything in. Feel more comfortable mentally. Sometimes I get quite a bit of anxiety being the center of attention and thrown in the middle of things, etc I like to see how other people behave, etc Then think about in conjunction with how I act. Life is a never ending learning experiment.
srp_clean_factor2_criminaltendencies_13_: It would have to be literally life and death and even then I wouldn’t feel good at all about that. That’s money that they’ve worked hard for. Hours of work and time. I would feel guilty taking any of that. So more likely than not I would seek other options. Loans, savings, etc. Something, anything outside of begging or manipulating someone. That wouldn’t sit right in my conscience at all. I’d feel so terrible. It would be tough to live like that. It’d be like having a weight on my conscience. Bad feelings all around. I’d likely try and pick up a side gig that could pay out really fast.
srp_clean_factor2_erraticlifestyle_11_br: I lean on the side of taking a little more time, but it also depends on the situation and what type of person they are as well. Their own personality, etc. I don’t think not taking enough time is an automatic death sentence or anything. I think couples who jump straight into it can be successful as well. It all depends on the makeup of the two people. Every person can be different. Going slower with someone might not work as well as going slower with someone else. It’s nice to keep this in mind as well to calm nerves when you’re meeting someone new.

feedback: All questions were clear and understandable. I enjoyed the study. Thanks!
machiv_clean_interpersonal_tactics_6_bro: Yes, there is someone I trust completely, and that is my best friend. He is the one person in my life who knows all of my secrets, my deepest fears and desires, and all that other secret stuff that people keep close to their hearts. I have known this person almost my entire life, so there is a reason for the trust that I have ... it's been earned from many years of being in each other's lives. Otherwise, I think trusting most people fully is risky. I am generally more of a trusting person just because I like to see the best in people.
machiv_clean_morality_8_specific: I would be fully honest. There is no reason to be deceptive or to try and mischaracterize the situation. It is perfectly reasonable to ask for someone's help and it's also legitimate for them to refuse if they want to or need to. I would be honest because the truth would come out eventually, so what's the point of being deceitful to begin with? And if the first person I was requesting help from couldn't or wouldn't help me, I would ask someone else. Lying to achieve my goal, in this instance, would be pointless and self-defeating. I would feel good about my action because it was honest and forthright.
npi_clean_factor0_na_10_specific: I am always willing to listen to someone else's idea or perspective and I am never so locked into my own approach that I won't consider other options. I believe thinks happen most easily and most effectively when people work together toward a common goal. And I believe the old cliche that ideas can come from the most unexpected places (and people)! In no way is it important for me to show that my way is the best. Unless it is a situation which has been preceded by a strong disagreement or conflict (in other words, if there were extenuating circumstances) I might feel differently. But as a rule of thumb, the best way is the way that works best.
npi_clean_factor1_grandioseexhibitionism: This is the case in my life to a reasonable degree. For the most part, my family is supportive and we are there for each other in good times and bad. So, yes, I've been praised on occasion, but I would say it's always times that I have deserved it. In other words, it's not empty flattery or false adulation. This is true of my friends, as well. We are all pretty nice and friendly and we care about each other, so I think (in addition to the usual teasing and joking that friends do) there is a lot of appreciation and good will, which includes compliments. I feel good when I receive these kind of comments, unless there's an ulterior motive that I'm aware of.
srp_clean_factor2_criminaltendencies_13_: I always try to be diplomatic, fair and patient first. But if the situation is urgent or the person is being especially difficult (for no reason), then I am not above making a pest of myself. I wouldn't go so far as to make threats, but I would apply pressure in the form of a consistent stream of communication (calls, emails, texts, etc.) and going to higher-ups, if applicable. But instances like those are rare (although when I have had to turn up the heat a bit, I'm happy to say I got what I needed!). I much prefer tact, politeness, and respect.
srp_clean_factor2_erraticlifestyle_11_sp: I don't usually like to do something like that with someone I've just met. I would want to have a proper date first. And I'm not sure how I'd feel about someone who would want to do that themselves. I like to work my way up to being physically intimate. I think doing it immediately after meeting someone sets the relationship (if there is going to be one) off in a weird way. It defines it through sex as opposed to through mutual respect, appreciation, and understanding. So, my reaction would be to exchange numbers and make plans to see each other again, at a later date, ON a real date!

feedback: hard work
machiv_clean_interpersonal_tactics_5_bro: Recently my daughter got upset because I voiced a negative opinioin about a sister in law. My daughter felt I had changed into a permanently depressed, grief-stricken negative person after the death of my wife (her mom). She felt that I was too negative and was starting to affect her children buy talking so much about death and dying. She was scared that I might try to kill myself, I have kept things on the sutface. Overall, this strategy has worked pretty well. I rea;ize it is not really honest, but it avoids strained relationships, so it is worth it.
machiv_clean_morality_7_specific: I would definitely choose the second option  First, I think it is best to present a balanced, more or less objective view about myself as it is, in my opiinon, best to tell the truth. I think it is misleading and dishonest for a person to overlook or hide their flaws and mistakes. If something happens down the line it could prove to be embarrassing. How often have we read news articles where a person was found out to be lying about their credentials? Second, by showing that I am a human being who makes mistakes, I show people that I am just like them. That creates empathy.
npi_clean_factor1_grandioseexhibitionism: This happened to me once. I took over the tasks of another employee who was not doing her job because of a personal matter. She stopped buying parts, thus impacting operations. After pleading with her to do her work my boss gave up and promoted her. Then he gave me her job, I bought a lot of parts. At a meeting, the operations manager thanked me in front of the staff for getting things back in order. I responded woth nothing more than a nod. I felt being humble was the best way to act. That way others do not get mad.
npi_clean_factor3_entitlementexploitativ: I feel it is better to let respect be earned over time rsther than to expect it. I took a job at a partronage-filled government agency in  1985. People were outwardly polite but they did not respect me. I could have demanded respect, maybe complained to our diversity department, but that would have alienated my coworkers. I chose to earn their reapect by working harder and better than they. I wrote better reports and offered to help others with theirs. I read up on the industry (mass transit) and used my knowledge to write better reports. Eventually I got everyone's respect.
srp_clean_factor1_callousaffect_4_broad: I am a big fan of fight movies, especially kung fu movies from the seventies. I have to admit I get really excited by physical violence. The kicking, the blows, the skilloful punches and htrusts really excite me. I loved the Bruce Lee movies, as well as the Kung Fu tv series, I really liked Kill Bill, directed by Quentin Tarantino, a true fan of violence. I am afraid to admit that it seems to be part of human nature to be violent. Or maybe it is cultural, not sure. Still, it gets my hair up to see a smaller person beat up a bully.
srp_clean_factor2_erraticlifestyle_7_spe: I am an Asian, the model minority that never makes trouble but quietly obeys the rules. I would choose to stay quiet about the rule change. First, I fear that if I speak out I might be targeted for retaliation by the system. I think the school system ia only looking for an excuse to do something to minorities. White parents have much more power and are thus able to speak out about things more readily than people of color. The whites know they cannot be pushed around so they can speak out about the system with greater freedom. They often do just that.

feedback: No comment.
machiv_clean_interpersonal_tactics_3_bro:
I have never found myself in that situation.  Having power, in and of itself, does not interest me.  I do my best to be sincere, though I know I fall short of that mark at times.  I would not like it if others flattered me to serve their own ends, or curry my favor.
This has never seemed as if it would be the right thing to do.  However, I do not work in a high-power business setting, nor in sales or marketing.
People can generally sense if someone is being sincere, or if someone is apple polishing.
While flattering those in power may have short term benefits, it could well backfire in the long run.
machiv_clean_views_of_human_nature_12_sp:
Given I do not know the coworker beyond brief, neutral interactions, I do not think I would have an opinion either way.  I would tend to believe the best about the coworker who was promoted.  My colleagues' opinions could be based on their experience, or they could be jealous of the coworker and seeking to discredit the person.  When I see someone succeed, I would generally assume it is due to merit, unless I had direct knowledge or experience otherwise.  I don't really give a lot of thought to the reasons for another's success, unless it impacts me directly. 
If an individual got ahead by engaging in office politics, the truth will likely come to light.  Or the person could lose their position by others playing the same game.
npi_clean_factor0_na_9_broad:
I think it is important to "read the room" before sharing stories with others.  I take the setting, the person's apparent mood and stance, and past interactions into consideration.  Being tuned in to someone's interest and engagement, by body language, stance, eye contact, would be good indicators of politeness vs genuine interest.  With good friends, close colleagues it is easier to gauge.
Sometimes people are quite engaged when I share stories, or at least appear interested; if I sense disinterest or boredom, I tend to shorten what I am saying and end quickly.  
Sometimes it is difficult to read others' reactions in these situations.
npi_clean_factor1_grandioseexhibitionism:
In this situation I would thank my teammates, and make it clear it was a team project, acknowledge others on the team as well.  
I would be sincere and grateful, but might use humor to deflect, as it is sometimes difficult to receive compliments, be in the spotlight.
Being gracious is important, as others are being thoughtful in taking the time to notice my contributions and make a point of sharing this.
Being grateful, acknowledging others and sharing the credit are all important when working in groups or teams on the job.
Most people need some encouragement or acknowledgement to carry on with sometimes difficult projects at work.
srp_clean_factor1_interpersonalmanipulat:
I would choose to stay fully honest.
Being dishonest violates my ethics.  In this case, stretching the truth or cherry picking certain experiences would likely backfire.
I do my best to maintain my own standards and ethics.  
I do remember as a teenager I pretended to be a sports enthusiast to get a job writing for a small weekly hometown newspaper; I was able to do the job as well as could be expected.  Nothing bad happened, but I did not do this again.
The truth will out, eventually.
Any opportunity that would come at the expense of integrity is certainly not worth it.
srp_clean_factor2_erraticlifestyle_11_br:
I am not interested in casual sex, and only once gotten into a sexual situation quickly, though this developed into a long-term dating relationship in the end. This was over thirty years ago; being someone depressed and meeting this person in a club, after several shots of gin, had a great deal to do with this situation.  This was the exception, as I get to know people, have many interactions, dates, before moving to a sexual relationship.
I am generally careful about intimacy of all sorts, and currently identify as demi-sexual, even asexual, at the present time.  The idea of sexual intimacy does not interest me at this time, though if there were a strong emotional connection I would not rule it out.

feedback: It's a lot of worrying and thinking, but everything went smoothly and directions were clear.
machiv_clean_interpersonal_tactics_2_spe: I'm on both sides of that debate. Everyone will always have to choose their own course, and even very benevolent persons will be at crossroads from time to time. I don't notice my guard up most of the time. That's going to be in certain circumstances and probably with lesser known people. I sometimes sense others treating me with mild skepticism and I welcome proving them wrong.
machiv_clean_morality_8_broad: I am struggling to recall a specific incident but do feel strongly that's occurred before in my life. srp_clean_factor1_interpersonalmanipulat: I am not in the manipulator club for sure. I get very resentful if anyone tries that tactic with me. feedback: Everything made sense? Most of the questions were pretty open-ended, I felt like I could say anything that was relevant.

npi_clean_factor0_na_9_broad: People usually have somewhat muted reactions to my stories in social situations, but I think that this is saying something about them more than it is about me. srp_clean_factor1_interpersonalmanipulat: I would feel pretty uncomfortable acting this way. I would feel like it could blow up on me at any moment. srp_clean_factor2_erraticlifestyle_11_br: I like to move slowly when it comes to these matters. I am more interested in finding someone who is compatible with me on a deeper level. npi_clean_factor0_na_9_broad: I think that people enjoy hearing what I have to say when I say it. I'm usually a person who is quiet and likes to sit back and listen to other people. npi_clean_factor3_entitlementexploitativ: I think I would speak up. It's important for people to know what you have contributed. srp_clean_factor1_callousaffect_4_broad: I dislike conflict and violence. Somehow I can be kind of empathetic about it and feel what the people are going through. srp_clean_factor2_erraticlifestyle_7_spe: I would probably do a lot of research. I would try to create a good argument with several different points that support my argument. But if it's something that I think will really affect me and people I care about then I'm going to speak up about it. It really just depends on the situation and how serious it is.

npi_clean_factor2_leadershipauthority_5_: I do not believe I am on a path to becoming a great person. I am retired so I am no longer making contributions to a workplace. srp_clean_factor2_criminaltendencies_12_: I have never felt the need to carry a weapon for protection. I live in a safe community. I have never had any situation where I felt threatened. srp_clean_factor2_erraticlifestyle_8_spe: I would not want to join in a risky activity. I would hold back from participating. feedback: The instructions are clear but I do find it hard sometimes to come up with specific examples from my own life.
machiv_clean_morality_7_specific: I would be transparent about set backs because this can have more of an impact on the listener to get a realistic view of the situation. Simply talking about successes as if there are no hardships along the way leaves people who may be looking for tips or inspiration with a false idea of how things are supposed to happen, which could lead to a lot of discouragement. It's important to see failures as a part of the process instead of something to avoid at all costs. I don't think this would ultimately damage my image, rather I think it would increase people's respect of me for my honesy.
machiv_clean_views_of_human_nature_11_br: I believe most people are fundamentally decent and selfish or cruel people are the exception, however it's still important to approach people I don't know with some amount of caution. There's always the possibility of coming into contact with one of the exceptions. An example would be person who tries to become an online friend of yours, claiming mutual friends in common and then they end up being someone else entirely and taking advantage of you in some way. Another is a person who only associates with you as a way to connect with others to further their own goal or career. Although nothing very significant has happened to me in this area I think I've become somewhat more skeptical over time.
npi_clean_factor1_grandioseexhibitionism: I am often told this about my photography which flatters me and also reinforces that my hard work to become better at this craft has been worth it. I've been in meeting where I have explained why a certain idea of assumption is not correct and was not listened to because I did not have the correct 'status'. It was not productive to try to convince them. I've found it works better to explain my view later to individuals or sometimes to step back and let people find out on their own.
srp_clean_factor1_interpersonalmanipulat: I probably  would look for another person or way more aligned  with my views to accomplish that because other wise I would either be stuck having to continue to put on an act around this person or I would be caught in a lie and labeled as being insincere or opportunistic, and neither are a good out come. srp_clean_factor2_erraticlifestyle_11_br: I've had a 'one night stand' situation one time in my life and it is not for me. I do not feel guilty after but I do feel a bit empty. We met in a bookshop and then spent some time talking in a cafe and the next day I spent more time with him. The emotional bond has to be there for the spark to happen.

feedback:
A word counter on the page would help. I think I exceeded the mininum word count you requested on each page. But I didn't want to copy/paste it into a count program because I didn't want you to dectect anything that you might think was AI.
As for thoughts, I was surprised I was able to write so much off the prompt. It's probably because of the age bracket I am in. It makes me wonder how/if the young generation could do the same with out Ai/Grammerly/spellcheck.
machiv_clean_interpersonal_tactics_5_spe:
When presenting business feedback I try to be very factual. If there is a meeting where old ideas are questioned or new wild plans are proposed I will try to deferr the situation to allow for data to be gathered. I think a powerpoint presentation containing facts, finances and forecasted outcomes is the best way to decide between multiple project choices.
If I am forced to provide feedback immediately I would give my true thoghts on the situation but also state that there are always alternatives to be explored and I be happy to look into them. Then later after doing more research I would document the conversation and my recommendations via email to all people who were in the meeting. I would also include in the email people who are involved with the project who were not in the meeting so we all can be on the same page. 
Formally documenting discussion in a email keeps it clear what was said. Later if the project fails because management went against your recommendation you can refer to the email instead of saying "I told you so".
machiv_clean_views_of_human_nature_11_br:
I used to believe people are good and kind. That is true for myself. I only had a small circle of family and friends when I grew up. They were not teasers. As I entered the real world my first exposure to people who will take advantageous of you was through teasers. It started off as harmless teasing that wouldn't bother most people but I thought what they were saying was true and it confused and hurt me. This made the teasers laugh harder and seem to enjoy tormenting me more. That is when I learned people delighted in seeing how far they could take things. Then when they got caught they tried to say it was all just in fun.
Now I am cautious and skeptical around all but a few people. I give everyone new the intial benefit of the doubt but when they make the first wrong step or do anything questionable I exclude them future interactions with me if possible.
npi_clean_factor0_na_10_specific:
One of my favorite sayings is there are always more options than first appear. I welcome new ways of thinking. I especially like automation. There was a quick report that I entered a date range to run every morning. A manager challenged me to automate it. He said that the two minutes a morning I spent typing in the date range adds up over time and could be better spent. While I understood that to be true I didn't think it would really make that much of a difference in my day. Now I love the extra two minutes I have every morning and have since automated other more complex tasks.
When I colleague presents a new idea that is just the tip of the iceberg. It could improve the current process and also have applicability to other tasks. No idea should be dismissed without evaluation.
npi_clean_factor2_leadershipauthority_4_:
I generally like to be part of a team with an assigned task. However, there have been instances when I go into a meeting and see the orgnaization is overwhelmed. They don't understand the task that needs to be completed and the people that will need to be involved. Often they have underestimated the time and money needed to complete a project too.
I will sit back and listen as long as I can. Then I will try to make suggestions to put the leader on to the right path. Sometimes the leaders are so full of themselves and cocky they reject the breadcrumb of help I am throwing them. Othertimes they realise I am a key ally. When they realize how much I can help them my status on the project gets increased. This could be a good or bad thing. I like that management realizes I'm a team player that can help different departments. But giving this project more help than expected takes away from my other work.
In some cases the person leading a project has beem an ill suited choice or just plain incompetent. In those cases I have gone to management to ask for a leader change. A few times I was promoted to lead.
In summary I prefer to be part of a team but won't sit back and watch someones incompetence ruin the business. In those cases I will step up to lead.
srp_clean_factor1_callousaffect_6_specif:
I did have a friend from high school that I ended the friendship with after 10 years. She had strong stances on what she thought was right and wrong. I think that is a good thing. I prefer to be around people who have opinions even if mine differ. It leads to good discussions. 
One day she made a comment about disabled people. This was a topic that I don't believe we had every talked about before. What she didn't know is my son was being evaluated for possibly having autism at the time. I was emotionally fragile and her comment hit me the wrong way. I was silent and didn't tell her how upset I was.
It wasn't uncommon for us to go long periods of time without chatting. I just didn't respond when she contacted me again. About 10 years went by after that and she reached out by email. We had some email chats and were supposed to gettogether but something (can't remember what) happened and it got canceled. Neither of us has reached out again.
I feel bad and missed her. Now that we are both adult empty nesters I can imagine all the fun we would have because we both like to travel. But I feel bad that I ghosted her back then. I don't think she know what comment set me off. But I think if we got back together knowing her true feelings towards disabled people would haunt me.
srp_clean_factor2_erraticlifestyle_7_bro:
One of the tenents of law is that ignorance of the law doesn't allow you not to follow it. I'm a rule follower. Where possible I like to know the rules before going into a situation. I will read the fine print of contracts and understand my obligations.
As long as the rules I am expected to abide to are documented I will follow them. If I don't like the rules then I shouldn't have got myself into a situation where they apply. For example, carrier contracts on airplanes or boats say that you must obey any rules given by the captain. This is designed for your safety as a passenger.I can't stand people who don't read the rules and become disruptive in public situations.
Aside from breaking my parents curfew rules when I was a teenager I don't think there have been times in my adult life where I have deviated from legal expectations.

feedback: questions were rathered generalized
machiv_clean_morality_8_broad: I have always tried to be completely honest in voicing my opinions and justifications for a particular response in various situations without any concern for whether or not it would increase or decrease the acceptance of my position. The goal should always be in getting the job done in the best possible way irrespective of the outcome on you personally. Using compelling reasons should always have a background that support them and their effects on others. I always give the real reasons to back up up my stance and that has always seemed to work the best. I use valid statistics to justify my feelings and position on the issue.
machiv_clean_views_of_human_nature_11_sp: Losing your wallet in a public place would be an absolute disaster with the loss of cash the least of my worries. The amount of information kept in your wallet, licenses, credit cards, personal information could be disasterous in the long run. Whether someone would return your wallet wouldprobably be a 50-50 chance in most circumstances depending on where it was lost. If it was lost in a restaurant there would be a very high likelihood of someone finding it and returning it or turning it in. On the other hand if it was lost in a subway station I think there would almost be a zero chance of getting it back.
npi_clean_factor2_leadershipauthority_4_: I would only volunteer to take on the leadership role if I felt that I had an extensive knowledge and experience about the particular situation that I knew I could contribute and make the group more effective in completing it's goals. If I felt I didn't meet the criteria I would do everything possible for them to pick another leader from the group or to search elsewhere for someone who has extensive experience in either managing volunteer groups or who has knowledge about the particular cause for the comunity project. I really have no experience in working on community projects and feel that their has to be others out there with knowledge and experience that would be ideal for the situation.
npi_clean_factor3_entitlementexploitativ: In most situations you really shouldn't expect something in return but rely on doing the best job that you can in dealing with others and always trying to maintain high standards. Your contributions should be directed at the job at hand and not necessarily worry about supporting some miscellaneous aspects that don't contribute to the overall resolution. I always try to help provide some resolutions that contribute to the discussion but stay on track without expecting to get something in return. It also depends on the importance of the particular situation and the necessity of providing a resolution. With friends and colleagues I never really expect anything in return for my contributions.
srp_clean_factor1_callousaffect_6_broad: With friends the most important aspect has to be loyalty and an emotional connection rather than mutual benefits or goals. Over the years (and I'm old now) I've lost many friends because they've changed or lost their jobs, moved away or unfortunately died. I've never developed a friendship simply based on their usefulness to me or my family. I've never ended any friendship on any other basis and if they contacted me after years have gone by I would still respond to them as a friend no matter how much time has gone by. In my opinion friends you have developed are for a lifetime or not just for some convenience at the time.
srp_clean_factor1_interpersonalmanipulat: I was the head of a college department (emergency medical services) for almost 40 years and I've seen many different faculty members all with different aspects about how to handle the management of their jobs. Some were very effective in managing their classes and clinicals while others had their preferences that made it difficult to schedule classes. For example some didn't like giving lectures while others didn't feel confident in supervising hospital clinical sessions. I never tried to make them do more tedious tasks than were capable of which would cause failure and unhappiness for the students. Some didn't push back and others were literally a pain in the ass.

feedback: The instructions and questions seemed clear to me -- Best of luck with your research!
machiv_clean_morality_7_broad: Yes, I have found myself in such a position, where doing the right thing wouldn't be the most advantageous to my long-term goals. One particular example that comes to mind is an error I made at work that a coworker who was higher up in the hierarchy of the company assumed somebody else had made. At first I let him believe that was the case, but once I returned to my desk I was wracked with guilt for being dishonest in a way, and not only that, because a coworker's reputation might suffer from an error that I made. In the end I went back to him and pretended that I hadn't realized I had made the mistake originally, but then told him I double-checked and the mistake was actually mine. I didn't want to admit that I was being dishonest at first, in order to preserve my reputation as much as possible, but wouldn't let a coworker take the fall. It seemed a good compromise in my mind at the time.
machiv_clean_views_of_human_nature_12_sp: My first instinct is usually to assume the best from people, and I know that people can become suspicious and jealous without actual cause. Since I don't have any evidence to suggest this isn't the case, I think I'd rather assume that people are good because it enhances my experience of living and working with other people. In most cases, I assume that somebody's success is due to some combination of merit, good luck and a bit of strategy but believe it can rarely be reduced to a single trait. I also have tended to work at jobs where somebody who is not pulling their weight would be called out on it, so I don't worry too much about undeserving coworkers moving ahead since they tend not to make it in the long-term anyway.
npi_clean_factor2_leadershipauthority_4_: If I had to choose between these two options, I'd want to be the person giving directions since I have found at times that my ideas can be more helpful than even some people who have been in a managerial role for a while. If it is a competent and good leader, I also don't mind taking instruction, though my most ideal way to work is without having to delegate or manage others and without a supervisor, and this is usually where I tend to shine, with problem-solving and self-direction without the distraction that working within an inefficient team can generate. And again, I don't necessarily chafe at the idea of having a leader as long as they are competent, but unfortunately I have had many experiences where there have been bad, rude or ineffective leaders where I would have preferred to lead myself and when I am in a position of leadership myself, I tend to give people autonomy and respect and think it leads to a better working environment in a lot of cases.
npi_clean_factor3_entitlementexploitativ: I often don't expect the same in return, though if they were a competent coworker, I would try to welcome it. I tend to not want to inconvenience people even when I need help, despite trying to be helpful myself and despite knowing that a lot of people welcome the opportunity to help others. The other thing I have found is that sometimes accepting somebody else's help can lead to things being even more difficult than figuring things out yourself, since then you have to focus on effective communication which isn't always easy. But yes, if somebody genuinely wants to help, I try to let them, though I do think it's also nice to do things without an expectation that somebody does something nice in return.
srp_clean_factor2_criminaltendencies_14_: I have rarely and probably never felt the urge to do something physically aggressive toward another person and would never threaten somebody with violence. That said, I do have a lot of anger sometimes and when it comes out, I can be physically expressive and tend to do things like strike inanimate objects, swear or even on rare occasions hit myself. Even after these instances, I almost always feel weak or ashamed, like I'm an out of control baby or something, though I imagine it would pale in comparison to how awful I would feel if I ever was violent toward another person, since I think there are few things as despicable as that and that there are really few excuses for acting in that way toward another person, especially when in a vehicle where you could cause real harm.
srp_clean_factor2_erraticlifestyle_10_sp: This really depends on the experience and the specific set of rules. My tendency is to bend rules if there is no clear reason why something shouldn't be done. However, if this was a group of friends where somebody could get in trouble for us bending the rules, or where somebody could get genuinely hurt, I probably would be less inclined to do that in a group activity since I would never want my friends to get hurt or in trouble. If it was just me engaging in a similar activity, I would tend to be more risky and more willing to break rules, as I have a bit of a thrill-seeking side and think it's better to ask forgiveness than permission for a lot of things, plus I believe that a lot of rules aren't very well explained and so in those cases, think it's okay to push things a bit and keeps life interesting.

feedback: I thought this was a very interesting survey. It really made me think through the situations. It was a great exercise in evaluating how I handle difference scenarios.
machiv_clean_morality_7_specific: I would choose the latter and use the modest, transparent account of my experiences. If you acknowledge your success, along with the setbacks you have faced, I think that is something more people can relate to. Everyone has setbacks and challenges. This will help you connect better with the audience and they are more likely to listen to your presentation. The former approach is not realistic and will not be something others can relate to. It will also be dishonest and manipulative. People will likely shut down or tune out and the message will be lost. It's a very narcissistic approach.
machiv_clean_views_of_human_nature_11_br: I think the majority of people are good and will do the right thing. However, there is a section of society who are completely out for themselves. They will use others and hurt others, just to get what they want. I have learned to identify these types of people, through experience. They only talk about themselves, use manipulation and don't care about anyone else. I try to stay far away from those people, when I encounter them. I wasn't always good at identifying these types of people, but it was something that I learned over time. I've also learned that there are a lot of people that want to do the right thing and help others.
npi_clean_factor0_na_10_specific: If the other person has an idea that could also work, my first thought is, how can we work together to make this even better? In most cases, when problem solving, I want to find the best way to find the answer. I don't care much about getting credit or pushing my ideas on other people. That isn't the purpose of the work. The work is to find the best answer and work together to solve the problem. Many problems have more than one solution. I don't like it when others discount my proposals and try to push their ideas on me. I wouldn't want to make someone feel that way by doing that to them.
npi_clean_factor1_grandioseexhibitionism: I do not like to be the center of attention. In big gatherings, I do not talk to groups of people and I am not the life of the party, by any stretch of the imagination. I usually talk to individuals or couples. machiv_clean_interpersonal_tactics_5_bro:
I was trying to break up with my boyfriend at the time. The breaking up had been going on for a few hours. He was threatening to kill himself and had barricaded us inside out apartment. This is in the days before cell phones so running into the bathroom and dialing 911 was not an option.

Even though he wasn't threatening to harm me, I needed to get that situation under control, so I lied and told him we would stay together. He went to sleep and I stayed awake in bed all night. When he was at work the next day, I moved out and as far as I know he didn't kill himself.
machiv_clean_views_of_human_nature_14_sp:
I think people are more attached to people than to what they own, however both can have repercussions that are more wide ranging. Almost all of us expect to lose a parent; as adults that's usually the way it works, so to some extent or another we are prepared for it.

The loss of a large amount of money can send a person into a tailspin. If they were relying on that money for income to sustain themselves and their family, they may not be able to continue to afford to get to work, pay their bills or provide healthcare for their loved ones. So while losing a parent can emotionally be damaging initially, the long range effects of losing money can be more burdensome.

feedback: The survey was clear and thought provoking. It made me think about the various scenarios and why I feel and respond the way I do. It also made me think about goal setting and deadline management and made me realize I am at one end of the spectrum and others may be more casual about deadlines and goals. It made me try to "get in their head" to see why they are like that when it is a foreign concept to me. It was interesting!
machiv_clean_morality_8_specific: I would do a balance of the two. I have been on both ends of this exact scenario at work. When I was working, I was responsible for branch openings in a 3 state area. They were very intense. At times I needed to ask for help from my colleagues and at times I needed to jump in and help them. We tried to work as a team because it made the opening go smoother and made us all achieve our goals for a smooth opening.
machiv_clean_views_of_human_nature_9_bro:
I think each person is different. Some people are self-motivated and others need the external pressure to stay on task. Those that are self-motivated set tasks and goals for themselves and stick to it. They typically make sure that those goals align with external goals to either meet or exceed them. I am more that type of person. I set my own goals and make sure they align with any external deadlines or requirements. In fact, I typically make sure they are well within the deadlines so there is no last minute pressure. 

Other people are not self-motivated and rely on external goals and deadlines. In fact, if there is a 5-to-10-day deadline to get something done they only focus on the 10 days and generally do it later rather than sooner. 

I think that people fall within a range of totally self-motivated and totally externally motivated. Most people fall midrange. I fall to the extreme side of self-motivated. Some fall to the opposite end and often struggle to meet deadlines. 

There is no one reason why people differ. Sometimes it is how they are wired, how busy their life is, or some don't feel the pressure or importance of goals and deadlines.
