These are people that have never done anything to break it in my 40 years of life so i pretty certain i can completely trust them. I think once you know someone and know what there motivations in life are it makes it easier to know if you can trust them. Living wouldn't be as rewarding if you couldn't trust anything or anything at all. I fall in the middle. I'm neither over trusting or suspicious of everyone. I proceed with caution and usually give people trust until they break it or let me down in some way. If you dont trust anyone at all its harder to form meaningful relationships and bonds with others.
machiv_clean_views_of_human_nature_14_sp: People are in generally more emotionally attached to people that they love and care about. Most people would do anything to bring back a loved one. Heck, people even spend lots of money just to save their pets lives when they are sick. When it comes to a family member I value their life over material things. Money / valuable property is something you can always get back but if a loved one dies that is forever. I'm generally not very materialist in the first place and if all my possession were suddenly gone it wouldn't be the end of the world and something i could get over.
npi_clean_factor0_na_9_specific: Anyone who is my actual friend and enjoys being around me would probably enjoy hearing a story i told. It would be the same way in reverse. I really not a big story teller. I generally do more of the listening but when i do share its because the story is worth sharing or context relevant. So, of course my friends would enjoy what i have to say or why would they even be hanging out with me in the first place. I don't really consider myself a great story teller but i usually keep my stories short and to the point so not to bore anyone or so if its not interesting we can move along
npi_clean_factor3_entitlementexploitativ: I personally strive to be someone that tries to focus on others but it's not easy thing to do. I generally don't think of people as objects or try to get anything out of a situation. or person. When there is a balance i think that's when relationships flourish. I feel the less someone has the more open to helping and assisting them we should all be. I tend to do more for people who i feel have less or going threw a rough time. There is something satisfying about doing something for someone with zero expectations. Not everything's needs to feel like a business transaction or how to get the most out of a person or situation.
srp_clean_factor1_callousaffect_5_broad: When it comes to violence or combat related sports or activities they are something i'm not really a big fan of. Especially as we learn more about traumatic brain injuries and the long lasting impacts. I would prefer if we would move beyond these activities. Things like bullfighting are pretty disgusting to be honest as the bull really has no say in it all. At least in boxing and other combat sports the athletes consent to it. I have no issues with violent movies or TV shows but they aren't the first types of movies i would look for. I have no issues watching violent movies with my friends. It's a movie and its not real so i personally don't object. I just fear the impacts these violent games/films/tv has on the younger generations that consume it.
srp_clean_factor1_interpersonalmanipulat: This question is pretty difficult. I think it is also very depended on the situation. In most cases I think its a good thing to stick with what you believe and not just be a yes man. Unfortunately, that isn't something that is always possible. In a perfect world we could stand by our beliefs and hold strong but sometimes making a small concession in your beliefs will put you in a better spot later. Usually when im in situations like these i'll push back a little but also acknowledge any good talking points the other person had. It really depends on the person. Generally people with very strong beliefs aren't going to listen to what you have to say so just agreeing a bit and getting what you need is sort of the correct play.

feedback: Nothing was confusing or unclear.  Very thought provoking questions.
machiv_clean_interpersonal_tactics_3_spe: I don’t believe that flattering someone in power is the right thing to do in general.  I believe it can gain you brownie points and smooth roads for future gains but morally, I do not believe it is right if wha you are telling them is untruthful.  Complimenting someone and truly believing that they are “really talented” or whatever compliment you use is fine if one truly believes that person is truly talented.  However, if the compliment is given only to get on their good said/gain favors, I do not believe it is right as it is being deceitful and lying.  Common courtesies and politeness is fine.  Even being star struck by someone is also acceptable but laying on compliments just to benefit oneself when the admiration is not true, is deceitful and selfish.  In general circumstances, I do not use or believe flattery should be used if it is not truly felt.  I cannot think of an instance off the top of my head where I feel it would be morally right.
machiv_clean_views_of_human_nature_9_bro: In my experience I have found that most people need a carrot or a stick to be motivated to do something.  I rarely find people that are self motivated and that want to work hard just because of some inner drive.  Most people need to have something like a carrot (for instance a paycheck or reward) to work - especially a long-term, daily commitment for multiple hours on end.  Or, on the other hand, they need a stick - a punishment or threatened punishment to motivate them to do something.  An example of this would be a teenager getting grounded for not keeping their room clean.  I would guess that the carrot/stick motivators are probably necessary for 80% of the population.  I do believe there are people who are motivated just by pride, passion or internal goals but those people are more unique and/or wealthy enough to be able to “not work a day job” to pursue things that make them happy, achieve goals they would like to reach or challenge their abilities.
npi_clean_factor1_grandioseexhibitionism: In general, it is not important to me that others notice my achievements.  If there is a conversation going on that involves something I excel at, I would probably joint mostly because I am so interested in the topic that I enjoy talking about it.  I would probably start asking a few questions, for the most part keeping it low key.  However, the questions I would probably ask would slowly reveal that I know q lot about the subject and would begin to reveal that I have expertise in the area.   I would not try to make my experience stand out, however, I would not deny it either.  Others would probably notice and ask me questions bout my experience and I am sure I would enjoy talking about it with them.  While feeling a bit prideful about my accomplishments might be a temptation, there are always others that know more/perform better, so I would not want to set myself up to look foolish.
npi_clean_factor3_entitlementexploitativ: I believe everyone should be given respect because they are a human being.  I also believe people can lose other people’s respect by their behavior.  I think that everyone should start out with the same level and manner of respect and it is up to each of us to continue to keep/earn it.  How we treat others, how we speak to people, whether we act morally and thoughtfully and kindly towards others can greatly influence how much respect others give us.  If I don’t feel that I am being treated with the respect that I deserve, I first check myself to confirm I am not getting basic respect.  If ithat is the case, I will speak up for me or on behalf of someone else stating how I should be treated.  I feel we should be careful with that and not push it too far to think bigger of ourselves and expect special treatment.  It is nice to get special treatment but it often should not be expected.
srp_clean_factor1_callousaffect_6_specif: If I suddenly came in contact with an old childhood friend that needed help, I would most likely try to help them - at least to a degree.  It partly depends on what type of help they need.  I would not resist helping them just because they can’t offer anything in return.  I would be more likely to resist helping if it was something I thought was going to end up hurting them more in the long run or something I could not see through to the end so it would not end up helping them after all.  Sometimes it is really hard to know whether to help someone or not.  I would hope that if I helped this friend that we would be able to continue to stay in touch and keep the friendship going.  However, if it felt like they were just using me to get something for themselves, I would be less likely to help.  Partly knowing whether I help someone is the feeling I get in my gut.  If it feels “off”, then I would decline  and move on.
srp_clean_factor1_interpersonalmanipulat: In general I don’t believe most people are easy to fool.  I think there are certain topics that people might be more vulnerable to falling for a scam - like finances - but in general, that is motivated by a desire to  obtain something they don’t already have.  I think most people who have lived in the world can see when something is “too good to be true” or will quickly learn that.  When we are young, I think we can be more easily manipulated because we haven’t learned to take things with a grain of salt, or not just trust everyone.  I believe most people in general are thoughtful and cautious.  There are always exceptions to the rule, of course.  And, there are some people who are incredible at manipulation, but in general, I think people tend to be difficult to fool.  Because of this, I try to be straight forward and honest with people because that is exactly how I want to be treated.  I want to be able to trust what another person is saying.

feedback: Everything was clear. Thank You.
machiv_clean_interpersonal_tactics_4_bro: I think it would be very difficult to live with complete honesty and still maintain personal relationships with people. Most people are not used to complete honesty and would consider this to be rude or disrespectful. Fairness would also be a very hard trait to live by. It would fly in the face of loyalty. You would be perceived as two faced and not to be trusted. Universal kindness would also run into problems. There would situations where that would be used against you and you would be taken advantage of. There are also occasions where it is better for the other person to be left with a dishonest agreement that leaves their emotional well being intact.
machiv_clean_views_of_human_nature_10_sp: I think there are certain situations on the job where corners need to get cut to stay in line with the resources that are available at the time. To become complacent to cutting corners and not know the difference of the things being routed can be recipe for disaster. I think it is much better to default to the ethical behavior and procedures and to only stop following these protocols when absolutely necessary. It is very important to not lose sight of the boundaries even if they need to be crossed from time to time. Nothing is ever black or white, but we need to understand the need for these considerations. Sticking to a set rule of ethical behaviors is going to be a better operating principle in the long run.
npi_clean_factor1_grandioseexhibitionism: I have been complimented on various aspects of my personality and capabilities. I a have never been very good at the social art of accepting these with gracefully. I have to admit they are appreciated. My mind does on occasion reflect on these accolades with fondness. I think that these are easy ways to help people maintain a degree of emotional health. A heightened sense of self. I wish I was better at communicating my appreciation at the time of the compliment. I regret that I don't reciprocate these situations better. I think it might be a byproduct of how I was raised. This wasn't something I experienced often as a child growing up.
npi_clean_factor2_leadershipauthority_4_: In this situation I would find myself wondering what type of contributions I could bring with me if I was to take charge of this project. If I felt that I was in a better position to carry this carry this project to it's goals, I would seriously consider taking a leadership role. If I found there to be a sort of competition for leadership, I would step back and try to ascertain what I would do differently and why. If I felt that another volunteer was approaching this role with the zeal and competence I felt necessary to accomplish our goals, I would find it easy for me to step back and help get it done.
srp_clean_factor1_callousaffect_6_broad: I think a lot of the friendships I have had in my life that drifted away were because our lives took different paths. I do not like them less for this. There is a lot to be said for the usefulness of a friend. Having someone you can count on if you need a ride or a few bucks until payday, but at the end of the day I think loyalty is my clear winner. Having a friend that sticks with you and can see your side of a situation is one of the great benefits of friendships. It can help one's mental well being in difficult encounters. It can be a pedestal to build one's self esteem on.
srp_clean_factor2_erraticlifestyle_7_spe: If I was given a new policy that on first consideration I felt I disagreed with my first reaction would be to want to see it's implementation across the organization. This would give me a better understanding of the effect it will cause to procedure. If I found that this was unnecessarily burdensome or even harmful to our goals I ask for clarity on it's intended function. If this was not addressed adequately I would question the policy and the person who implemented it. With this mindset I would look for ways to decrease the rules grip on my procedures. Maybe going as far as sneaking around it if necessary.

feedback: I wish you added a word counter. I didn't like that rule! Without a word count instruction, I'd feel freer in my responses.
machiv_clean_interpersonal_tactics_1_bro: It is sometimes justified, because in some cases the end can justify the means, especially if the end goal benefits the greater good. Another instance could be as simple as concealing the reason behind your actions because you want to surprise someone with a special treat, such as a special anniversary dinner or a surprise visit to a grandparent. In other words, I think the answer is nuanced, depending on the situation. For a politician, on the other hand, honesty about motivation is generally essential to maintain transparency and trust. An exception there could be keeping something secret to protect national safety. That isn't a situation in everyday life, though. Balancing honesty with self-interest requires you to examine who benefits from being deceptive about motivation. If you genuinely practice such deception in order to protect someone's feelings, for instance, that can be justified.
machiv_clean_morality_7_specific: I would acknowledge both successes and setbacks in order to add realism and transparency, which I believe would be more engaging and relatable to the audience. If you downplay the challenges, the audience could view the 'victory' as diminished, because an easy victory is less impressive. Sharing the setbacks, moments of feeling discouraged and frustrated during your journey to achieve a goal is more relatable to the average person. It makes the accomplishment sound sweeter and more worthwhile. Minimizing the challenges makes you sould like a braggart, moreover. No one wants to listen to someone brag about successes, especially if they make it seem like their success was inevitable. In such a case, you might suspect the deck was stacked in their favor; if they always score easy wins, maybe they even cheat or pull strings. Overall, I would aim for a more honest accounting to gain the empathy of the audience and make my story about the journey of discovery and ultimately success, not just about winning.
npi_clean_factor2_leadershipauthority_5_: I hardly expect to be successful or famous, and I really don't strive for either of those outcomes, at least in the way most people view them. Success for myself mainly means not having a negative impact on the planet and on others; success means leaving the world just a tiny bit better, or at least leaving no destruction in my wake. But even this limited view of success is a challenge, which I concede and view realistically. It is always a struggle to make the right choice, and I generally struggle with any decision since I tend to consider the consequences, especially the risks and negative repercussions of my choices. Overall, I want to live a simple life with integrity rather than achieve greatness or fame. I am more confident of succeeding in that goal.
npi_clean_factor3_entitlementexploitativ: It's very hard for me to be generous of my time and effort, moreso when it comes to helping colleagues rather than close family members. I don't really ever expect a colleague to come through for me, and I have a terrible time asking for and accepting help, both because I struggle to feel worthy and because I want to feel independent and don't fully trust the help I get from others. On the other hand, it is of course admirable if someone provides help without expecting the favor to be returned. Still, I resist doing favors, and I'm even more reluctant to ask for a favor, even in return. I'd rather struggle on my own than ask for help; I think my dad instilled that in me, as asking for help was seen as weakness to him. But providing help without expecting it to be returned is the definition of altruism.
srp_clean_factor1_interpersonalmanipulat: I'm sure there have been rare occasions when I said what someone wanted to hear in order to achieve something, but that kind of behavior really goes against my grain. In such circumstances, the implication is that I really don't genuinely care about the other person. If the other party is a true friend or close family member, I prefer to be straightforward since deception only undermines our relationship. On the other hand, if the other person is my work manager, a not-so-close neighbor, or someone involved in a commercial transaction like a car salesman, I'd be more inclined to choose my words and disclose only what will help me reach my goal. For instance, at a work function I might tell my boss her idea sounds good -- especially in front of others -- even if I have some doubts. Expressing those doubts openly could jeopardize my job, chance of promotion, office politics, etc.
srp_clean_factor2_erraticlifestyle_10_sp: While I generally feel automatic resistance to authority and rules, especially rules that seem nitpicky or overbearing, this scenario sounds like the rare case where I would be inclined to adhere to them. If the rules are in place for public safety, for instance rules regarding mask wearing and vaccination, I don't have a problem following them and instead have a problem with those who resist. Anything that is intended to prevent risk to life and limb, I don't have an issue with adhering to. This goes for speed limits, seat belt rules, bicycle safety, etc. Overall, I would consider whether the rule actually contributes to safety and reduces risk, or whether it's just nitpicking. The scenario would play a big part in my response to the rules and authority. Is it a situation potentially fraught with risk? Or is the risk being exaggerated to justify the onerous rules?

feedback: I thought everything was fine and clear.  Not confusing in any part.  This was a very interesting study!
machiv_clean_interpersonal_tactics_4_spe: I would interpret that person's behavior as a reminder that people are complicated.  Someone can appear kind and honest, yet still make choices that serve themselves, even if those choices aren't outright wrong.  I don't think it's possible to be entirely good in real life because everyone faces pressure and compromise.  Still, I would get disappointed when someone who seems morally upright (say the preacher or my therapist)makes self-serving decisions in secret (if I somehow found out about it).  It feels like a betrayal of the image they project (Tammy Faye Baker and her husband Jim and Jimmy Swaggart come to mind). For me, honesty means matching words with actions, and when they don't, it damages trust and makes their goodness seem less genuine.
machiv_clean_views_of_human_nature_12_br: I believe most successful people do not achieve their positions through hard work and integrity alone.  Often, they rise because they know the right people, hold influence, or even use secrets as leverage to get what they want.  Success in today's world is frequently tied to power, connections, and strategies that are not always fair or moral.  Despite this, I would personally choose to live a moral life.  Cutting corners or acting dishonestly might bring faster results, but it creates harm and inequality.  Real success should be measured by integrity, fairness, and the positive impact left on others. I really wish it were the only way that people tried to succeed.
npi_clean_factor1_grandioseexhibitionism: At social gatherings, I usually approach situations with some caution.  I might quietly walk up to someone who is not already deep in conversation, or linger on the edges of a group, waiting for the right moment to join in.  Although I don't often admit it, I do enjoy being the center of attention once I feel comfortable.  If there are tables, I'm happy to chit-chat with those seated nearby.  When I get up for food or drinks, I try to use that as a natural chance to connect with others doing the same, keeping things casual and friendly. I think to stay to myself or to not attempt speaking to others would make me look odd and weird, and I don't want that.
npi_clean_factor3_entitlementexploitativ: In that situation, I would feel frustrated, disappointed, and hurt.  I'd pause to assess the situation calmly before reacting.  If it felt safe and productive, I might assert my contribution respectfully, stating what I added and offering to help implement it and support the team moving forward.  However, if asserting it could cause unnecessary conflict without benefit, I'd let it go and focus on what I can control, like continuing to do good work.  I have to weigh the pros and cons of doing so.  I know I can contribute by helping the others.  I'd also give myself some self-encouragement. I try to balance validation of my own efforts with maintaining relationships, staying mindful, and avoiding impulsive reactions.
srp_clean_factor2_criminaltendencies_14_: I have definitely felt the urge to act out physically when frustrated, especially in stressful situations like driving.  Sometimes, I've wanted to throw things and scream, but I haven't actually acted on those impulses.  Instead, I try to notice the anger (without judging myself), and remind myself that acting aggressively could make the situation worse or put me and others in danger. I often use deep breathing or grounding techniques, like focusing on the FEEL of the steering wheel or counting breaths to calm down. Afterward, I usually feel relief that I didn't give in, but also a lingering sense of tension or frustration.  Managing those urges requires patience, self-awareness, and sometimes using coping strategies to calm down safely.
srp_clean_factor2_erraticlifestyle_8_spe: Because of the p; potential consequences and risks involved, I would not join in on these types of dangerous activities.  Even if most people in the group tried to pressure me into participating (maybe teasing me with names like "wimp" or "fun-killer"), I would hold back.  My safety and well-being are far more important to me than giving in to peer pressure.  I consider factors like the seriousness of the risk, my comfort level, and whether proper precautions are in place.  While the thrill might be tempting, I know that the potential for injury or regret outweighs any short-term excitement.  And I sure don't want to end up on YouTube in a video about people dying doing stupid things.

feedback: Thank you for the opportunity to participate. I hope my responses will help in your research.
machiv_clean_interpersonal_tactics_2_spe: I almost always trust someone until or unless they give me a reason not to, at least as far as things like lying, cheating, and stealing. But at the same time I have a tendency to be wary of people's motives, as I have been hurt and betrayed by others after taking them into my confidence. For this reason, I am usually on my guard about what I share with others about myself unless I feel like I know someone very well, and even then I may be cautious about what I do and say around them. I also tend to take what people say to me with a grain of salt, especially if something sounds implausible or too good to be true. By the same token, I do not completely trust anyone's reactions to things I do and say, sometimes not even my own husband. But although I don't think I can say I trust anyone completely, I would trust my husband with my life and with my deepest secrets. I come closer to trusting him 100 per cent than I do with anyone else I have ever encountered or known.
machiv_clean_views_of_human_nature_13_br: I think most people are brave at least to an extent, although some people don't feel they are particularly brave. Those people, however, most likely have a certain amount of bravery inside that would come out when faced with a situation where it is needed, such as defending a small child or animal. Everyday bravery could be something small or seemingly minor, such as a shy person working up the courage to speak before a group or even to approach a stranger to ask for directions. It could be something larger, like assisting someone who is in physical danger or has been injured. It also takes a great deal of courage for an abused spouse to confront and/or leave their spouse or for an abused child to tell others what they are going through. I don't personally see these things very often and I don't know exactly how I would behave if I were faced with the need to show courage and bravery. I only hope that I would be able to summon whatever courage lies within me to handle a situation to the best of my ability.
npi_clean_factor0_na_9_specific: When I share stories in a social setting I usually get mixed reactions. Oftentimes while I think a store is amusing or funny, others for some reason may not see it that way. Occasionally I know if I was hit by hearing someone's genuine laughter, but other times the laughter or even chuckling may not ring true. I don't often share stories with others, especially when I am out with people in a group setting. This is because basically I am a timid person and I get uncomfortable if I am the center of attention. But it is also because of what I previously mentioned, that either my story isn't as entertaining as I think it is or I do not tell it very well. I also have a tendency to go overlong in my storytelling, sometimes giving too much background information and details that may confuse or bore the listener.
npi_clean_factor2_leadershipauthority_5_: I am 70 years old and at my age when I think of the future I think more about how I will survive my remaining years than what I could become in that time. At this stage of my life I believe I have become all that I can be and was or am destined to be, although it is true that life does throw surprises at people when they least expect it. I am no longer really on a path to becoming anything, good or bad, and I don't see much chance of something happening to change who I am, although I am always open to unexpected opportunities that I may not foresee.
srp_clean_factor1_callousaffect_4_specif: I do not even like watching a boxing match and I would be horrified to see two people in a fist fight. Part of me might be frozen where I am and watch although I would not want to, and part of me might want to either depart the scene or go try to find someone to intervene and stop the fight. So far I have never been in that situation so I do not know for sure how I would react or what I would do. I have heard but not seen neighbors fighting but since they are out of my range of sight I may listen out to see if the situation is bad enough for me to call the police, but when that does happen it is usually over before I decide if I should call or not. I think in most cases it is best to let the fighters handle it themselves while trying to be aware that if someone is in danger of being killed or seriously injured that I should call 911.
srp_clean_factor2_criminaltendencies_12_: I have never owned a weapon such as a gun in my life, and only once did I carry a canister of pepper spray. Many years ago a friend who was dating a police officer gave me a police-grade canister of pepper spray and for the most part it sat in a basket on a shelf. On one occasion I decided to carry it with me, I've forgotten why now, but I was more nervous about accidentally setting it off than I was worried about needing it. Occasionally if I am alone in an area that makes me nervous I carry my keys in my hand with one key protruding, as a friend who taught martial arts for women had suggested, as a way to jab someone in defense if the need arose, which so far it never has. What I usually do if I am feeling apprehensive while walking alone in an unlit area is to call my husband on my cell phone when he is at work and talk to him until I am in a safer area. If he does not answer or is busy with a customer and cannot talk I have a "fake conversation" on my phone so that anyone nearby will think I am speaking with someone. So far I have been lucky and have never been accosted or attacked, and I continue to have faith and trust that I never will.

feedback: Everything was clear
machiv_clean_interpersonal_tactics_4_spe:
This person sounds like a real hypocrite to me, and hypocrites really annoy me. That's how I interpret this behavior. I do think it's possible to try and be good in real life, but your actions and words must be the same both in public and private. It doesn't mean you are perfect and not exempt from occasional "compromising." Being honest is a moral that a person can strive for. It is one I strive for. I am the same person in public as in private, or at least I try to be.
If someone appears morally upright but is not that way behind the scenes (a hypocrite), then I want nothing to do with this person. Sometimes it is disgusting when people live two lives and try and be a "goody two shoes." I had a coworker who sucked up to the boss and always volunteered to do extra work. While not at work, she would bad mouth the company and the boss. That's a hypocrite. I avoided her.
machiv_clean_views_of_human_nature_10_br: I don't think it is ever justifiable to cut corners in order to get ahead because it is immoral and unethical. "Cutting corners" can also lead to bigger transgressions because I think you get into the mindset of "if I can get away with something small," then this could lead to committing even bigger less-than-ideal practices. Also, if you are cutting corners in a competition, it is no longer a fair competition. I am thinking of athletes who use steroids to improve their performance. That is wrong, even if you think "everyone does it." Personally, I am sometimes tempted to stay ahead by cutting corners, but I don't do it. That doesn't mean I am perfect moral person. I just believe honesty is the best policy and I try and live by that. I believe that if I ever did "cut corners," it would come back to haunt me. I might get into a lot of trouble and regret trying to take a shortcut in the first place. I think it's better just to stick to ethical behavior so you don't have to worry about bad consequences later.
npi_clean_factor1_grandioseexhibitionism: Like I said earlier, I am a shy and unassuming person. Sure, I love to receive compliments (who doesn't?), but I am embarrassed by them. I don't like it when I am the center of attention. If I receive a compliment, I thank the person and try and move on. Sometimes I try and joke and say that yes, I truly deserved the recognition, but that doesn't always go over well. If I am a part of a team, I would try and deflect the attention on me and comment on how I was just the part of a team. I would then try and stress that it was a team effort and give recognition to others who probably worked just as hard as I did or even harder.
npi_clean_factor2_leadershipauthority_4_: I am definitely not a leader. The thought of leading a group terrifies me.  I would much rather receive directions. I have been that way all my life because I am shy and probably a little insecure. I don't feel like I have the qualities needed to be a good leader. I was in charge of a fundraising group years ago. I had no idea how to organize or manage people. The fundraising flopped, and I felt like a failure. It does not bother me when someone else is in charge or a group, even if I don't agree with how they are handling things. I stay quiet and follow directions. I figure if something goes wrong, then I am not to blame. It also takes a lot less thinking to be a follower and not a leader.
srp_clean_factor2_criminaltendencies_13_: No, I would never pressure someone to give me money. That is wrong on many levels. If I were dumb enough to get myself into debt, then I have no right to try and use a guilt trip on someone to get money. I would choose to handle the situation in another way. First, I always manage my money, so debt would probably not happen. But if it did, I would try anything myself, from getting a second job to trying to get a loan, first. If that didn't work and I truly needed to ask someone for money, then I would come right out and ask them for the loan. I would not use manipulation. I would try and work out terms that might benefit them, such as adding interest. You should never pressure a person, even if you're desperate. It shows that you don't have much character. I think if you were honest with a person about your need, they would be more willing to help you out.
srp_clean_factor2_erraticlifestyle_9_bro: I am a very boring person and do not pursue things that are bold and wild.  I prefer routine and familiarity. I have always been a person who is content with an ordinary life. I am terrified at the thought of doing something daring, such as jumping out of a plane with a parachute. I don't even like to go to the top of skyscrapers because I am afraid of heights. The only "spontaneous" thing I did was toilet paper the band director's house when I was in high school. A group of us really did not like him. That was the only "bad" thing I did in high school. Luckily, it rained that night, and he had a real mess to clean up. At the time we did the deed it was invigorating, but I constantly worried that I was going to get caught and punished. Luckily he never found out, and that little act of deviance still sticks in my mind, decades later.

feedback: The instructions were clear. No questions were confusing.
machiv_clean_interpersonal_tactics_4_bro: I do not think it is possible for someone to live with complete honesty, fairness, and kindness, because there are moments when one has to compromise one's values to get what one wants or sometimes there are circumstances beyond one's control which forces one to have to compromise on their principles. Therefore I think that it is impossible for someone to live with complete honesty, fairness, and kindness. When someone appears morally upright but makes self-serving decisions behind the scenes, I feel really frustrated and annoyed because that person is being a hypocrite, and I detest people who are hypocrites. When they behave that way, they are in effect lying to others and being dishonest.
machiv_clean_views_of_human_nature_12_sp: My first instinct is to believe that they got ahead because of their hard work and strong ethics. It is because I prefer to give this coworker the benefit of doubt. I feel that it is unworthy of me to believe that the person got ahead by playing office politics, flattering supervisors, taking credit or others' work, and strategically avoiding blame, without having any proof. When I see someone succeed, I do tend to assume that it is due to strategy because I believe that one has to strategize to succeed, and that merit by itself is simply just not enough for one to succeed.
npi_clean_factor1_grandioseexhibitionism: I would join the conversation only if I can find a natural way to segue into it. Otherwise I would not bother. I would not try to make my experience stand out, as I find that to be self-aggrandizing behavior that I do not personally approve of. Instead, I would prefer to keep it low-key, as I am not in the habit of trying to make others be impressed with my achievements. It is not at all important to me that others notice my achievements because I do not feel the need for others' validations in order to feel good about my achievements.
npi_clean_factor3_entitlementexploitativ: I do not think that it is important to make others respect me. Instead, I feel that respect should come naturally over time. I do not think that it is natural to make sure others respect me. I feel that it is unnatural to expect such a thing. Instead, I prefer that I earn someone's respect over time. Usually when I feel I am not being treated with the respect I deserve, I try to let it go and not have it bother me, because it is not worth the time, effect and grief to be bothered by such a thing.
srp_clean_factor1_callousaffect_4_specif: I would do nothing, because it is not worth the effort to try and stop the fistfight. The two people will eventually tire and would most likely not be able to hurt one another significantly during the fistfight. I would feel worried about the participants getting hurt while watching it. It would be an upsetting experience. I would not find it exciting because I am more worried that someone would get hurt. I would also be likely to just walk away from the fight because I don't like watching people get hurt. Still, I hope that someone would try to stop the fight instead of everyone just watching the fight.
srp_clean_factor1_interpersonalmanipulat: I feel that it depends. Some people are generally easy to fool, while other people are generally difficult to fool. This belief does not influence the way I interact with others, because I do my best to be honest in my encounters and interactions with other people, and do not feel the need to fool other people to gain an advantage over them. I want my interactions to be one of mutual respect. Interacting with other people with either the mindset that people are generally easy or difficult to fool is just not the way I operate. It feels like a really exhausting way to live.

feedback: The instructions were clear. I did not find any of the questions confusing. It would have been helpful to have a word counter for the writing portions. Would have been easier to make sure I met the minimum word count. I thought the writing portion had very engaging questions. I always knew what to write. It wasn't an issue meeting the 100 word requirement.
machiv_clean_interpersonal_tactics_6_bro: Yes, there is one person in my life who I completely trust. That person being my mother. It's interesting because growing up I didn't fully trust her. Maybe because we had a rocky relationship at the time, but as I got older things changed. I think there's a different dynamic with parents when you are younger vs. when you are older. You realize they only want the best for you later on in life. You also get to know them on a different level that's not possible when you're a kid. You are almost on the same level and like good friends. I do think that fully trusting someone can be a problem, but not if that person is family. I am a little suspicious of others, but not my family. You realize over time that family is everything and what matters most in life. The level of support and trust is unmatched.
machiv_clean_views_of_human_nature_14_sp: It's kind of interesting because someone close to me just lost their parent so this dilemma hits close to home. I'm currently thinking a lot about this type of question. I would say that each person is different. Some are more attached to people while others are attached to objects. I don't think there's a definitive answer to the question. As for myself, I'm not as concerned with material things. Losing a loved one has a much bigger impact. It hits me really hard. Things are replaceable. People are not. The more time you spend around others, the more you miss them when they are not around. I can't really say the same about objects.
npi_clean_factor0_na_9_broad: This is another scenario where the answers are going to differ from person to person. It depends on the kind of person someone is. For example, I have a family member who loves to hear himself speak. He'll talk all day long. This type of individual is an example of someone who is often ignored. I'm sort of more on the introverted side. When I speak to someone, it's going to mean something. I don't just talk to hear myself speak. That's not to say I can't hold a casual conversation. But I would say people are interested in what I have to say because there's something more to it. They know I'm not just going off and talking just to be heard. They are almost always engaged and respond back. It's not the same when someone talks all day long. They are often ignored or the conversation is very shallow.
npi_clean_factor3_entitlementexploitativ: I would probably let it go. If some team members dismissed the idea entirely, it probably wasn't that great of an idea. I was probably very biased because it was my own idea and I had worked so hard on it. It's better to let things go and not dwell on them. Makes working as a team a lot less stressful. It's probably very likely that the other team members idea was better overall. I would be a little annoyed that my contribution wasn't even recognized though. I did put in the work even if it wasn't a great plan. Would make me much less likely to bring up ideas in the future.
srp_clean_factor2_criminaltendencies_12_: I can't say that I've ever felt the need to carry a weapon for protection. I don't put myself in those kinds of situations in the first place. I am the kind of person that avoids risky places altogether. Even if I was in this type of situation I would definitely be with a group. There is power in numbers. That always makes me feel safe. I think it's just common sense to avoid dangerous areas. I get that some people don't have that luxury. So I guess I am just lucky in that way. There have been a few times where I was unknowingly in a bad part of town. I only knew about it afterwards. But I still never felt the need to carry a weapon. I was just very alert and with a group.
srp_clean_factor2_erraticlifestyle_9_bro: I think doing things out of the ordinary once in a while is a good thing. Makes life interesting and takes you out of the mundane. I am always a little hesitant about doing something wild because I am a little self conscious. I am always nervous and apprehensive at first. Getting into it is the hardest part. It's somehow coming up with enough courage to do it that's the issue. Once I'm going for it and active, I feel a little better. I'm in the moment. I can remember crowdsurfing while at a concert. I was really into the show and just went for it. I felt so alive and invigorated afterwards. I wanted to do it again!

machiv_clean_interpersonal_tactics_1_bro: I think the reasoning behind not sharing the real reasons for doing something depends on the relationship you have with that person. In my romantic relationship there have been times in the past where I lied about a reason I did something. Usually it was because I didn't think the action was wrong however I knew it would start a fight and I wanted to avoid fighting with my significant other. However, that is something we have had issues with in our relationship so we have been making a conscious effort to not lie to each other no matter what. I don't think it's justified in a romantic relationship to lie no matter what. If you can't be 100% open and honest with your partner, the relationship will suffer.
machiv_clean_views_of_human_nature_14_sp: At first this seems like a really obvious answer but depending on the person, I believe it is possible for someone to become emotionally attached to their items. Yes, a loved one can never be replaced but for some people, neither can a large amount of money or property they worked their whole life to obtain. So yes, depending on the context of the property, I think the same amount of loss can be felt for both losing a person and for losing property. I've never lost either one that significantly affected me so honestly, I can't answer that question completely. It would depend on who I lost.
npi_clean_factor0_na_10_specific: I strongly believe the saying, "If you believe you're the smartest person in the room, you need to be in another room." I believe there's always something I can learn from others. There is nothing in this world that I know more about than another individual and there's always room in my brain to learn something new. I am not interested in showing my way is best. I'm interested in solving the problem in the most efficient way that will benefit the company. I would definitely share my solution and ask for feedback on it but then I would ask about their solution and hope that we can figure out a solution as a team.
npi_clean_factor3_entitlementexploitativ: I really think respect should be automatically given but on the basic of levels, especially with people who are just interacting or are acquaintences. When it comes to family members, romantic partners, coworkers, that level of trust should be there, however if that trust is broken, it's much harder to build back up in closer relationships. I am not big on insisting on getting the respect I deserve. I am not assertive enough to make sure I'm being respected. It takes a lot to get me to speak up about being disrespected. By the point I do speak up, I'm usually angry and not much gets accomplished.
srp_clean_factor2_criminaltendencies_14_: I'm not proud to say I have felt aggressive on the road at times. I'm not really sure what it is about driving that brings out the worst in people, but it does. I generally start out by yelling at people and giving them an angry look. However if they return my anger, I generally start to feel more and more angry and I will feel the urge to get really close to their car or ride their bumper if they cut me off. I generally take quite a while to calm down from those kinds of experiences though so it usually leaves me feeling pretty terrible for quite some time.
srp_clean_factor2_erraticlifestyle_7_spe: I would respond initially by discussing the new rule with my peers to find out what their stance is on the situation. If the majority of people I ask agree with me, I may go speak with a supervisor or someone who had the capability of either challenging the new rule or by giving more information as to why the rule has been put into place. Understanding the rule may help me better accept it. I am generally a rule follower however I have been known in the past to break rules that I truly didn't believe the reasoning behind to be valid. I am still fighting this rule and although it's taken a while to get past the consequences of breaking the rule, in the end it all worked out.

feedback: Thanks!  Everything was all clear.
machiv_clean_interpersonal_tactics_4_bro: I do not think it is possible to live with complete honesty, fairness, and kindness.  You can try, but people are flawed and will make mistakes.  No one is 100% good.  Everyone has a little gray in them and are going to do things that upset other people.  It would be impossible to think otherwise.  When I see someone appear to be morally upright but makes self-serving decisions behind the scenes, I see them as the biggest hypocrite.  I would trust them even less than someone who does things bad sometimes.  I find people those types of people more trustworthy than those who appear to be good all the time.

If I did amp it up, I would feel bad about having to lie about it.  It would make me feel like a horrible person.
npi_clean_factor1_grandioseexhibitionism: I do not enjoy showing off my successes or talents at all.  I am the type of person who wants to stay in the shadows.  I hate any kind of added attention.  I don't want anyone knowing anything about me like that.  I just feel like it's bragging in some sort of way.  I think social media has made me hate that type of thing.  I don't have very many successes so maybe I am just insecure about myself but I don't like it.  I wouldn't want anyone to ask me about my achievements or take notice of the things I have done in life.
npi_clean_factor2_leadershipauthority_4_: I would definitely not feel comfortable in a leadership role.  I barely know what to say to strangers who try to talk to me in a store.  I wouldn't know what to say to a group as a leader.  I would be horrible at it.  I have no problem with following directions from someone else, I prefer that route.  I am someone who can take direction well and don't feel like it's beneath me or anything like that.  Some people are better suited in leadership roles and others are better in the follower role.  That's just the way life is.
srp_clean_factor2_criminaltendencies_14_: The only time I ever feel like that is when I am trying to fix something that isn't working.  It just makes me want to throw the object against the wall.  I've never done anything like using my car to scare anyone on the road.  It's just a small burst of anger and I never actually throw the object. Sometimes you just need to let out some frustration.
srp_clean_factor2_erraticlifestyle_8_spe: I would be extremely nervous about this.  I don't like taking risks like that. No amount of peer pressure would get me on a cliff, a dangerous trail, or in a race car.  I don't care what I look like to other people, I am not doing it.  I am a very nervous type person and it would make me uncomfortable to be in these situations.

feedback: The questions and instructions all seemed pretty clear.
machiv_clean_morality_7_specific: I try to always be a humble person.  I don't like to brag about myself and I usually don't feel like I have achieved as much in life as I would have liked.  When I do have to talk about myself around people that I don't really know I try to be humorous and poke a little fun at myself.  I try to make more light of my failures and short comings in life than to brag about things that I may have accomplished.  I would try to talk about accomplishments and failures in ways that I can relate to others.  We all have successes and failures in life and I think it is good to look at both in light hearted humble ways to appreciate them more.
machiv_clean_views_of_human_nature_12_br: I think most people that get to the top or just even get to be managers or supervisors usually do so by cutting corners or bending the rules.  In my job experiences managers and supervisors are usually the ones that do whatever it takes to get a promotion.  They are often the most self centered employees that tend to disrespect all others to make themselves succeed. Instead of actually doing productive work the thing they do the most is self promotion and keeping their foot stuck in the door of people higher than them. Usually the most honest, humble, and hard working employees get stepped on and they tend to just come to work diligently and try not to stir up waves.  They don't tend to get promoted because it serves management best to keep them where they are. I try to put in for higher positions when they come open at work because I need to look out for myself and make as much as I can.  However I'm not going to kiss up to management just to move up or step on other employees that may be more deserving than me just to get a promotion because I don't think that would be fair.
npi_clean_factor1_grandioseexhibitionism: When I was growing up I was definitely more shy than now.  However I did find it pretty easy to make friends somehow. I think others usually seemed to pull me into their groups more.  My parents were both big talkers and very social so I think as I get older I've definitely become more sociable.  I am much more of a talker now than ever.  At a party or large gathering I still sometimes tend to be shy and like to blend in to the background but it usually doesn't take too long to strike up a conversation with others.  I think I'm a good listener so it also doesn't take much to get other people to start talking to me either.  However I still prefer smaller gatherings with friends with 3 or 4 people as opposed to large parties or gatherings.  I don't always like being the center of attention.
npi_clean_factor3_entitlementexploitativ: I don't need to demand recognition in a group at all unless it affects something like a grade in school or pay at work.  I don't need recognition in and of itself.  In college I always hated having to do group projects with people you don't really know.  Someone always seemed to want the recognition or to do things their way and no one else's.  That's fine as long as a get a similar grade for what I contribute too.  At work it's the same.  I would rather blend into the background but if someone seems to demand a promotion or to make more money when I am just as deserving or hard working then I have a problem with that because I don't think that's fair to me or others.  I will let things go to a certain extent but if I feel I'm losing out or being treated differently I will speak up about it.
srp_clean_factor1_callousaffect_6_broad: I think loyalty is much more important than usefulness. I'm not a trendy person or someone that drops friends and family when they are no longer needed or useful.  I have never ended a friendship but I've had a few close friends that moved on to something else or other people.  Recently I had a cousin that was very close and a best friend that ended our relationship.  We used to go to the movies, go eat, and shop on our days off but she met a man on line dating and stopped doing anything with me. I was very hurt by this because she completely stopped talking to me and missed my last two birthdays.  I think people nowadays just look out for themselves and they do what suits them with complete disregard to how it affects others. I don't think that's a good way to treat your friends that have stuck by you for 50 years.
srp_clean_factor2_criminaltendencies_12_: I have considered buying a gun for home protection but they scare me. I never carry a weapon with me.  I try to stay away from areas or situations where a weapon would be necessary. I always try to stay alert and keep an eye on things around me.  If I feel uneasy I try to leave for a safer location.  I always keep my house and car door locked especially when alone.  If possible I like to go places with others.  I feel like carrying a gun could go badly.  You can't really just shoot someone because you feel unsafe by law and someone else could take it from you and harm you with it.  I would also be too worried about it somehow going of accidentally too and hurting me and someone.

feedback: I thought everything was clear, although a lot of the prompts were a little too vague to give more detailed responses. In other words, the context of some of the prompts would have affected my answer quite a bit.
machiv_clean_interpersonal_tactics_6_spe: My answer strongly depends on the person, because I've known people who I trust implicitly and those who I'm comfortable sharing with but who I know to be a gossip. I've also had experiences in the past where I've told someone something only to have somebody else find out about it. It also would depend on what it is I'm sharing, because there are some things that I don't mind being "out there" as far as being told to other people. I still have to build trust with someone before I feel like sharing something personal with them, but that doesn't mean I'm suspicious of them. Most of the people I know who I'm close to are those who I've had to warm up to over time and I don't see that changing anytime soon.
machiv_clean_views_of_human_nature_11_br: My view has always generally been that people are basically good but it depends on their life circumstances whether they behave that way around others. People mistreat each other primarily out of fear for themselves or their loved ones, but their primary drive is to take care of someone - even if that someone is themselves. I've found that most people are friendly and sociable and that those who don't fit into those categories are more of an outlier, and even then there are a lot of grey areas where people reside. The more people I meet, the more they tend to follow that same pattern.
npi_clean_factor0_na_10_broad: The way I compare myself with others depends on context, because there are some skills I possess that I've worked very hard at developing and know that I'm farther along than my peers. There's no reason for me to compare myself to others when I'm confident, just like there isn't much advantage to comparing myself when I'm not feeling confident because I already know that I likely won't stand up to others. It greatly depends on context, but there are a lot of instances where I find myself seeking out information from others because I've witnessed them performing better at things firsthand and want to get better at those things myself. Either way I have to make that comparison, though in that instance it's for self-improvement.
npi_clean_factor3_entitlementexploitativ: I wouldn't expect this person to offer the same level of support just because I'm going through a tough time, but I would hope they'd at least be willing to try. Knowing that someone is there for me and willing to at least listen to my concerns is the bulk of my worries most of the time. I wouldn't say that I offer support with the expectation that I receive something in return, but I feel like treating someone respectfully and offering my time and advice would warrant something similar if I were to ask for it. It wouldn't be because I felt entitled to it, but more than we had that shared experience together and that we're both capable of supporting one another when the need arises.
srp_clean_factor1_callousaffect_6_specif: In this situation, I don't see any reason why I wouldn't at least lend an ear to my friend's problems. I would want to offer support and advice where I could, but it would definitely depend on what he was asking for. It would also depend on the circumstances for our friendship dissolving, though if it were simply growing apart, I wouldn't see any reason to shun him for it. I don't see any version of this playing out where I would ignore him or refuse outright, though if he were asking for something unreasonable (like a lot of money), I would have trouble helping out, at least unconditionally. But I also wouldn't blame him for things not going his way if he were putting in an honest effort.
srp_clean_factor2_erraticlifestyle_9_bro: I generally avoid "wild" activities but I don't mind periodically seeking out unusual or novel experiences for the sake of it. The times that I've done something spontaneous seem to be relatively mundane things but also things that I hadn't sought out or planned to do beforehand. One time I was on vacation when I saw a line forming outside of a shop so I decided to follow it and saw that it was an ice cream parlor, so I ended up buying ice cream even though that wasn't my intention when I went out that evening. Even as a low-key activity, it felt fun doing something on the spur of the moment, even if it wasn't a "wild" activity.

machiv_clean_morality_8_broad: I had a coworker who had a second home in a rural area. She talked about the peacefulness of nature and the delight of seeing animals in their natural habitat. She laughingly encouraged me to drop by if I was ever in that "neck of the woods." As it turned out, I also knew someone else who had a second home in the same countryside town. I wanted to get more information about the town's standard of living, the size of homes and the quality of local activities. I told my coworker that I would love to visit her since she spoke so enthusiastically about her weekend "escapes." However, I was really spying on an acquaintance to get a better picture of the life he was living so I could "size him up" better. I never revealed my true motives to my coworker who thought of herself as being in a position of a gracious hostess.
machiv_clean_views_of_human_nature_11_sp: I find it likely that someone would return my lost wallet because I have returned a wallet. I found the wallet on the second-floor patio of my house. I didn't know how long it was there. Someone had taken the wallet after an argument with the wallet's owner. The person who thought of this revengeful act ran away after the argument, tossed the wallet while fleeing and believed the rightful owner would never get it back. However, the loss was temporary. The wallet had money and forms of identification. It turns out that the wallet owner lived two blocks away from me. However, we were strangers to each other. The wallet owner was grateful and thanked me over and over.
npi_clean_factor0_na_10_specific: I learn from other's perspectives because I can't think outside of my own box. Capabilities are based on individual and shared experiences. I want to enlarge my box. There was a project involving the baking of scones to judge the best one. At first, I and others decided it was up to the individual baker to choose any scone recipe: from online, family favorite or history. It seemed like a normal procedure in a contest. However, a colleague suggested that all bakers use the same recipe. I assumed that wasn't any kind of real competition. However, I was told there are choices within the same recipe regarding brands of ingredients, individual ovens, room temperature, amount of effort and types of mixing. I agreed that's true. As it turned out, the baked scones were not identical. There were big differences in appearance, taste and size. Variety became the dominant feature. My consciousness was raised without any yeast or leavening.
npi_clean_factor3_entitlementexploitativ: There should always be a high level of politeness between people. I find that listening and responding to others is a way to expand basic courtesy into a more meaningful interaction whatever the setting. If I can think of something funny to say or answer in a way that makes other people laugh, then I can begin to melt away any awkwardness or preconceptions. Humor, and it's not always easy to think of something, levels the playing field. The ice (or frostiness) melts allowing all to be more relaxed and open. Humor is assertiveness in disguise. It guides the conversation and the subsequent responses.
srp_clean_factor1_interpersonalmanipulat: I applied for a job to review business contracts although the work I had done was with corporate annual reports. I got the job and found contracts to be hard to navigate. I did make myself sound more appealing, but it ended up that I didn't fool anyone, especially myself. At some point I was thanked for the work that I did. Me feeling was that there was more work, but it was not given to me. I learned my lesson and never padded my resume of cover letter to make myself a more likely candidate. I was paid for the work that I did, but it made me more realistic and honest which was the true worth of the experience. Live and learn.
srp_clean_factor2_erraticlifestyle_11_br: I like friendships to evolve. I don't like any kind of leapfrogging, and I don't mean that as an intimate position or group sex. I think I might be a prude which is sort of kinky in its own way. However, the possibility of pregnancy, abortion or disease is a stronger visual than physical attraction. Spontaneity has to take a back seat, and I don't mean the back seat of a car. Reality and responsibility are better than regret, reality and responsibility. It is better to think you might have missed out on something. Time will tell if you wait for it.

feedback: All went well, thank you.
machiv_clean_interpersonal_tactics_1_spe: Individualized states need to operate in a meta-stable form that allows for developmental motivity. Thus an object of technicity must be developed so that the technological objective can be actualized. Such motivity can only occur without negating factors that become divergent and disjunctive. The object of course is analogous in the nature of things and is movable. The movement must be actual with verifiable principle and accomplishment, such movement is accomplished through process not through feelings and temperament. Any private personalized information is not part of the analogous construction of the object since the object of technicity is not for aesthetical purposes but for a development of an technological object, aka, the product of production.
machiv_clean_morality_8_broad: I would not embellish a situation for further gain than necessary. If one has to over extend themselves in such a manner it becomes begging. The person being asked has a choice, to apply pressure on the other will definitely transform that other to a position of an " otherwise Other" thus excluding them from the present moment and any possible future events. Anything with in reason would naturally be countered within a realm of the possible for the other, it might be not all one has desired but a partial is better than a nothingness. To exist in a nothingness is where desperation and intense anxieties occur that can lead to furthered damaged psychological states.
npi_clean_factor1_grandioseexhibitionism: Past successes are not present moment successes. Letting one's talents excel in the present moment is expected, reason being, that talent is easily translatable to effort put forth and commitment, which should not be compared to other people intrinsic vulnerability of inadequacy. Boast and bragging is a different shade. If one is confident and has real intrinsic value there would be no need to boast about past accomplishments. Past accomplishments or efforts are usually released during causal conversations unless its an interview then such past successes would have to be presented directly as a presentation. To be idolized for accomplishment only feeds a dark narcissistic of a personality.
npi_clean_factor3_entitlementexploitativ: One would expect some level of mutuality due to simple empathy and concern for another but that is not always the case. Sameness is unrecognizable, it is difference that which is recognizable. The two situations are different and always will be. How the difference was obscured in the first situation may not obscure the difference in the second situation. There are givens and then there are offerings. The person being asked for help may only be able to provide the givens with no ability or capacity to provide an offering, aka, solution for the situation. Blame can not be placed upon this person nor the burden itself. If one is going to give an offering expect nothing in return.
srp_clean_factor1_interpersonalmanipulat: True believer syndrome has it dangerous effects. Very easily things can become erratic, grandiose and obtuse. Within the syndrome itself there is no separation between what is actual and the virtual realm of confabulations. Such a person would tend to gossip a lot so as to constantly check their reality. This could become a compulsive trait and obsessional. Speech acts consisting of large portions of third person references and slogans attempting to cast a wider net to fill in blanks of their confabulatory tales. I would have minimal contact with this person a say the least amount as possible. The trust level would be very low.
srp_clean_factor2_erraticlifestyle_9_bro: There is a difference between dopamine rushes and adrenaline rushes. One more likely has a higher associated risk than the other. Nuance does not mean risking one's life or health to achieve a level of excitement. If the range of excitement is regulated and modulated within a spectrum of sensibility excitement/nuance can be achieved through the simplest movements, actions or thoughts. Boundaries are aesthetical that do not necessarily need a bodily materialization or grandiose movement to overcome to enter into stages of nuance. Creativity itself is a spontaneous experience that can be daring or unconventional that goes against the grain of life. And for me that is about as much risk I would take.

I short, I would try to be sincere by actually finding something to praise them for. I think being insincere, especially these days, is easily spotted by people and is a sure way to fail at personal and professional relationships.

feedback: Everything was clear.
machiv_clean_interpersonal_tactics_6_bro: I completely trust my mom. She’s always had my back, and over the years, we’ve built a bond that’s hard to break. With her, I feel safe sharing anything because I know she won’t let me down. But when it comes to other people, especially strangers, I think it’s smart to be cautious. Trusting someone fully too quickly can leave you open to disappointment, or worse, betrayal. You can never truly know someone’s intentions until you’ve seen how they act in different situations. I tend to trust people I know well, but I keep my guard up with new faces. It’s all about finding a balance between being open and protecting yourself. Trust is important, but so is being mindful of who you let in.
machiv_clean_views_of_human_nature_10_sp: I don’t think cutting corners is essential to getting ahead. Sure, it might give you quick results, but it often comes with risks—like getting caught or damaging your reputation. Taking shortcuts might help you in the short term, but it can lead to bigger issues down the line, like a lack of trust from colleagues or even bigger mistakes that could’ve been avoided. I prefer to stick to ethical behavior. It’s not always the fastest way, but in the long run, it builds a stronger foundation. I’d rather know I did something the right way than take the easy route and regret it later.
npi_clean_factor0_na_9_broad: When I share stories, I usually get positive reactions. People often laugh, ask questions, or even share their own experiences. I think most people do enjoy hearing about personal stories, especially if they can relate or find something funny. But there are times when I get the feeling that some people are just being polite, nodding along without much excitement. It’s hard to tell for sure, but I think it’s a mix of both. Some stories connect better than others. As long as I feel like people are engaged, I keep sharing, but I try to notice if they’re genuinely interested or just being nice.
npi_clean_factor2_leadershipauthority_4_:
If I’m being honest, I’d probably feel a little motivated to step up if no one else is taking charge. I’m not someone who usually jumps into the spotlight, but if things are starting to get messy and it seems like no one else is gonna lead, I’d probably just go for it. I like getting things done, so if it meant organizing tasks and making sure everyone’s on the same page, I’d do it.

If someone else does step up, I’d be cool with following their lead, as long as they’re clear and know what they’re doing. I’m good at supporting others if they’ve got a solid plan.
srp_clean_factor1_callousaffect_6_specif:
If my childhood friend reached out, I’d probably try to reconnect, even though things have changed. We had a close bond, and I’d feel bad just distancing myself without hearing him out. I’d listen to what he has to say and see if I can help in any way, even if it’s just offering advice or a listening ear. Sure, he might not be able to give much back right now, but friendship isn’t always about balance. Sometimes, it's about being there when someone needs it.

When a friendship starts to fade, I try to weigh whether the connection still feels meaningful. If it feels like it’s just drifting because of life changes, I might let it go. But if it still holds value, even if it’s just a smaller part of my life, I’d try to maintain it. It really depends on whether both people still want to make an effort, even if it’s in different ways.
srp_clean_factor1_interpersonalmanipulat:
I think people can be easy to fool sometimes, but not because they’re dumb. It’s just that a lot of people want to trust others or believe the best in them. So, they might not always see when they’re being misled. Most of the time, people have good intentions, but they can still get tricked if they’re not careful.

Because of that, I try to be as honest as I can with people. I don’t want to manipulate anyone or use tricks to get ahead. I think it’s better to just be real with people and build trust that way.

machiv_clean_interpersonal_tactics_3_spe:
I think it can be possible to use flattery in a positive way. If its someone you generally admire, even if the flattery is strategic, it is still honest. machiv_clean_views_of_human_nature_14_br:
For me its harder to lose a loved one, than something material. People are unique and irreplaceable. When they are lost, it leaves a large hole that can't really be filled. You will always feel the loss of their absence that has a shape only they can fill. Since both of my parents have passed away, they are not able to be replaced in my life. I can focus on other things, but I will always feel the loss, and wish that they were still there. Nothing will really take that away or make things better. Things can be more easily replaced in my opinion. You can always get something better or upgrade something, and replace it if it breaks with a new one. I know you can be attached to things or places and sometimes they can be hard to lose. But when a person is lost because they are so rare and there is only one of them on the Earth, the loss is felt more deeply and is not something you really get over. 
I think people are attached to other people and to what they own. It just depends on what they value more, and what they can't afford to lose. Some people will be devastated if their house they lived in for years is foreclosed on, and they lose it and all the memories and investment that goes with it. Others will feel the same about losing someone important to them. It just depends on what they value more.
npi_clean_factor1_grandioseexhibitionism: I haven't really had that many people give me compliments or tell me how talented or capable I am. I think it might be that I am trying to protect myself from negative attention or rejection. But I do appreciate it when someone tells me something positive about what I have done, and how they have noticed and want to point out some of my strong points. I will always thank them, and listen to what they have to say because I'm curious. I'm always surprised when someone notices something I might not know about myself or when I look at it differently than they do. I also like to see myself though other people's eyes to get insight that I might not realize about myself.
npi_clean_factor3_entitlementexploitativ:

In my opinion, this is the highest standard you can follow when interacting with others. Just like its better to give than receive. Giving is selfless ant puts someone else above yourself. Its a gift and something like that shouldn't come with a price tag for the other person of expectation to return the favor. machiv_clean_interpersonal_tactics_2_bro: I do believe that people are basically good. machiv_clean_views_of_human_nature_9_spe: I think in this situation, I would have to make a loose deadline. If the project is a long term project, depending on how "long term" is meant in this situation. I am going to assume a year is a long term project. Assuming that, I would say "This project is due in a year, here are some milestones each person needs to have by this date" that way it's loose enough that someone can work on it as leisure, but also has deadlines that each member has to meet as well. Maybe do monthly big goals and the employee can meet that specific goal how they want to. As for postponing tasks and how I feel about that, I think it is a individual motivation aspect. I know there are some people who will postpone things till the last minute without any structure, but there are also people that love structure and really take to something like that. So, people and how they postpone things is more of a individual thing since some do it and some don't. I don't think it is a common thing, some people are on top of tasks.
npi_clean_factor0_na_9_broad: When I share my stories to people that are in my life or in social situation, I tend to find that the people listening really care and want to hear more from my life and experiences. I think people like listening to me tell my life experiences and stories, no one has ever told me to shut up or seems bored whenever I say anything so I would take that as a good sign. I really feel like people enjoy listening when they are asking questions as the story goes on and not in a way that makes you think that they are not listening. He had a strange outfit" and then they asked "What was he wearing?" when I had just explained that. The situation really depends on the individuals. For a lot of people, to lose a family member is going to be easier to cope with in the long run because they're able to comprehend that dying is difficult for the living.
