feedback: everything was clear but it's important to note that not all situations are the same.  there are times when it's easy to make a decision and there are times when it is very difficult to know how to make a decision; or even if a decision is needed.  life can be very complicated.  I clearly remember the time I told my parents of a decision my wife and I had made.  I was shocked when they reacted with great sorrow and I felt very bad about it and I still do.  life can be very complicated and sometimes we have to make decisions without knowing all the facts of the situation.  I like to play things safe.  I can't remember the last time I made a promise because I hate it when people break promises.  I appreciate taking part in this exercise and I wish you the best.
machiv_clean_interpersonal_tactics_6_bro: whether the correct word is "cynic," or "skeptic," that's how I describe myself.  I wasn't always that way.  as I approach my 70th birthday, however, I find it very difficult to trust ANYONE.  to me, it's a matter of playing it safe.  it's easier not to trust people.  i'm thinking about whether there are any people I trust.  I think I can honestly say there are no people I COMPLETELY trust.  of course, some come closer than others. there are some people I trust a little and there are more people who I don't trust at all.  I think I became more of a skeptic the longer I found myself in the work force due to my experiences as a caseworker (fifteen years) and a residential counselor (fifteen years) in a "group home."  I was employed to help people who were in trouble.  I found that it was very difficult to believe what I was told by my clients.  but the fact remains that there is no doubt that I am a very suspicious person.
machiv_clean_views_of_human_nature_10_sp: first let me say that I retired in 2019 so I will use my final job to answer this question.  at this job I was a residential counselor which means I was working in a "group home;" in my case a "half way house" where the residents were drug addicts and/or alcoholics.  as far as whether cutting corners is necessary to get ahead; this is a tough question to answer.  I would say that it is NOT essential.  it's probably true that 90 percent of employees cut corners; some more than others.  I don't believe that a person has to cut corners to get ahead. in fact, I think that people who cut corners are more likely to get in trouble than other people.  cutting corners can be dangerous because it can end up with a person getting a reputation as a mediocre employee and sometimes that reputation is well deserved.  personally, I did cut corners at every job I ever had but I tried to do this in an intelligent way.  I would NEVER cut corners in an important situation and I certainly would not cut corners which took advantage of other people.  I definitely stick to ethical behavior most, but not all, of the time and the reason I do this is not because I'm a "goody goody" but simply because I don't want to get in trouble.  I just don't see the logic in putting myself in situations that can come back to haunt me.  a situation where I might have cut corners would be if I took a seventeen minute break when I was allowed fifteen minutes.  the fact is that I usually would not go over fifteen minutes but it's a situation where I might cut a corner or two.
npi_clean_factor1_grandioseexhibitionism: it's important that I have never had a lot of friends.  I prefer to have few, friends.  But the friends I have are very important to me.  it is also true that most of my relatives are deceased or live far away from me.  also, I am retired and have long completed any formal education.  that being said, I can honestly say that I don't recall being in many situations where I received compliments.  in my case getting complimented is rare.  I have found that paying compliments is something that most people simply don't do.  most of my supervisors, over the course of working at different jobs covering forty years, almost never paid me a complement.  when I got a "performance review" there were some nice remarks about the quality of my work. but as far as a compliment in a normal setting, I have not received many compliments; in fact they have been rare.  I don't think most people feel comfortable paying compliments.  i'm not sure why this is.  I am the type of person who DOES pay compliments.  I think sometimes people who receive compliments get embarrassed.  when I am paid a compliment I truly appreciate it; probably because it does not happen often.  I've been married for over 40  years and the days of my wife complimenting me ended a long time ago and I must say I hardly ever complement her.  I have a 34 year old daughter who I don't see a lot of but I DO complement her sometimes; for example I sometimes tell her that I think she is doing a good job as a parent.  but once again, I am a person who has very few friends and relatives so I don't find myself in social situations very often; and I like it that way.
npi_clean_factor3_entitlementexploitativ: in this situation, I certainly would expect a colleague who I have helped, to help me.  however if I really went out of my way to help someone, in an important situation, I would NOT expect the person to help me as much as I helped the person.  I wouldn't expect it but I would be very appreciative if I received a lot of help.  if I had helped someone, for example by offering support, I would think it would be only natural for that person to, at least to some degree, return the favor and I would expect that.  but I think it would be a bad mistake for my to expect a lot of help from that person.  I have been told that I tend to have "unrealistic expectations" and the truth is that I do tend to have expectations of people.  whether they are "unrealistic" or not, I can't easily say.  so when we are talking about "levels" of support, level is an important word.  I do NOT expect the level of support I give to be the same level of help that I received.  and it's important to remember that the situations may be totally different. if I offered support to someone going through a major health issue, that's much different than receiving help in picking up a bottle of soda for me at a supermarket.  so I guess what it comes down to is I would expect a person to help me if I had helped the person but I definitely would not be expecting the same level of support.  as far as not expecting anything in return; this is tricky business.  I feel most people DO expect something in return although they might not admit it. if I helped someone I would expect to be helped in some way in the future.  I think that's just human nature.  but I do think there are some people who actually would not expect anything in return but I have a feeling those people are few and far between.
srp_clean_factor1_interpersonalmanipulat: i'm a person who is more honest than most people.  I just think it's better to be honest.  as someone once said "when you tell the truth you don't have to remember what you said."  that's one of the wisest things I ever heard.  I NEVER try to mislead or manipulate  ANYONE.  I wouldn't feel comfortable doing that.  that being said, I do think that people are generally rather easy to fool because most people want to hear good things so when they are told good things that are misleading they tend to believe them. feedback: Your instructions were clear, and I didn't find the questions confusing.  It was a nicely constructed study.
machiv_clean_interpersonal_tactics_4_bro: I don't really think it's possible to live with complete honesty, fairness, and kindness. feedback: I thought everything was clear and straightforward.   The questions and scenarios were interesting.
machiv_clean_interpersonal_tactics_5_bro: I had a former boss that I would tell her everything she wanted to hear so she would go away and leave me alone.   She was not a nice person by design and my coworkers and I quickly discovered that it was just easier to tell her what she wanted rather than the truth or what we thought.  If we told her something other than what she wanted to hear, it usually ended up being more work for us or she would make things difficult for us.   The easiest path for everyone was to agree with her and tell her she was right.   It helped to avoid conflict in the group and kept everyone calm and peaceful.   While this was frustrating for several people on the team, we all realized that it was the better option given her reluctance to accept anything other than her own ideas.  To make things worse, the boss above her was the same way so this happeend across several levels in the company.   It was not an ideal situation and she ended up leaving the company.   After she was gone things improved significantly and we were able to implement some positive changes.
machiv_clean_views_of_human_nature_11_sp: I think there is a good chance my wallet would be returned.   Today we have tools like social media that we can announce when something like a wallet has been found.   In my community I see regular posts when things like wallets, keys, and phones are found.   Before social media it was much more difficult to share when an item has been found and locate the owner.   If my ID is in the wallet, it would also be easier for someone to contact me since I am on social media.   I still think the majority of people are good and would return the wallet.   This actually happened to me and I found someone else's wallet in the parking lot at the movie theater.   I drove it to the person's home to return it to them.   There are probably people that would keep it or take the money from it but my thought is those people are the minority.
npi_clean_factor2_leadershipauthority_4_: I prefer to be the one giving directions in most situations.  There are situations where I am perfectly comfortable with someone else taking the lead and I take direction from them.   When someone else is leading the group, they have my full support and I will do whatever I can to let them know they have my full support.   In those situations I put group success above my own and am wiling to do what I can to help the team.  I do feel comfortable taking the lead and feel that I do a good job.   When someone else is leading, I am happy to take the # 2 spot and do what I can to provide them the guidance and support they need.   I usually do not offer my opinion unless asked because I feel it is important to respect the other person's position.
npi_clean_factor3_entitlementexploitativ: I think it is important to let go of personal feelings and if you share ideas they should be considered.   If the group dismissed my ideas and suggestions, I would not take it personally.   The situation might be such that it wasn't the right time for my idea or it is something that would be considered later on.   Recognition is not something that I need in order to do my job or stay motivated.   It is something that I certainly appreciate and feel good about but it is not something that I need to continue working hard.   In situations where I feel my input or effort isn't respected, I continue to focus on getting the job done and realize that later on things might be different.   I also believe it is important to stay positive and focused on my work for the sake of my coworkers.
srp_clean_factor1_interpersonalmanipulat: I think people are generally easy to fool because people are often naive and very trusting.   People by nature want to believe others and will initially do so until that initial trust is broken.  This is very important because it does influence the way you interact with other people.   You want to share enough information so people trust and believe in you, but also withhold enough information so you aren't showing all of your cards at once.   I'm not a believer in taking advantage of people but I know it does happen.   There are situation where I don't think others are intentionally misleading people but they are not always cautious or suspecting enough.   When interacting with other people, I always take the approach "trust but verify" and that seems to work for me.   I believe in being as honest and as transparent as I can with others.
srp_clean_factor2_erraticlifestyle_11_sp: I think there are times where you just feel a connection and strong vibes with another person.   Assuming both people are sober and alcohol is not involved, it would be ok to see this other person.   It would help to know if the feelings are serious or if they are the alcohol motivating the feelings.   I would also consider things about the person and how they are acting.   For example, do I get the feeling that this is what they do with other people they meet or is this something more unique and genuine to their interaction with me.   If this person is known for going home with people then I would not pursue it any further.   I have no interest in being just another person.   Usually their intentions and actions are more obvious and you can easily tell what their motivation is.   I would also evaluate my own feelings and if this is just something impromptu or if I am genuinely feeling it too.   That would dictate a lot of what happens from that point on.

feedback: The instructions were clear.  Nothing was confusing to me.  I hope I answered all the questions correctly.  I tried my best to write down my own experiences and feelings.

With honesty, there is trust also.  They are closely related.  You want to create trust in the workplace so that people can feel they can be honest in any discussion or decisions that are made.  This is what feedback is for.  Helps find out what makes the team work.  Different opinions are normal and expected.  There is balance and compromising that results from disagreements.

Walmart makes a lot of money, they certainly can pay their employees better.  So this makes me think that lot of the people who own companies/businesses are not leading honest and moral lives.  Another example is CEO of United Healthcare.  They make so much money because they deny people's claims and don't want to pay what they need covered.  This is NOT living a honest moral life. They are doing whatever it takes to succeed, making money off people.  Restaurants, the waitresses don't get paid enough so the customers have to cover their wages by tipping. I do not feel capable than others because I like hearing other people's point of view or perspectives because I may not realize or learn something from those perspectives. Someone else may not think my way is the best which is why we all discuss it and decide which way is the best.  This is why companies feel diversity is good for the company because diversity brings different ideas and we all can work together.  Teamwork makes the dream work as they say.  I love learning about other people's perspective.  There is always something I hadn't thought of.
npi_clean_factor2_leadershipauthority_5_: I know some people feel that they are confident with their lives that they are destinated for greatness in their future.  Usually those are people with high self esteem.  I am not that person.  I don't have high self esteem so I'm more of one that sees success as something to work toward with uncertainty because I don't know if things will work out in my favor.  Life is unpredictable.  What is a path to be a great person?  I believe if you do the right things, work hard and be honest, that defines a great person.  I define success for myself if I follow all those things, honesty, morals, and do the best as I can then I believe I have been successful in that way even if the outcome isn't successful.  What matters is how it is tried.  I am never confident I will achieve success because I think there's unforeseen things that isn't predictable.  Life isn't perfect or predictable so I cannot be confident things will work out until it happens.  Once success is achieved, then that makes me confident and look back at what I did, makes me feel good that I have done the right things to get there.  I try to be helpful, honest, one with morals, do the right thing and hope for the best.
srp_clean_factor1_callousaffect_4_broad:
In the movies, the violence is acting so I just watch it with interest.  There was this show I binge watched last week.  Squid Game.  It had a lot of fights, conflict, gore and violence.  This interests me because I know it is all acting and not real.  People aren't actually killed or harmed so I have this sense of peace watching fights.  It's all action and I enjoy watching the violence.   

Fights in real life or online, if it's real, makes me grimace for the victims.  I sympathize anyone that gets hurts.  There are rare times that the person deserves the other end of the fight and it makes it feel like there's justice.  Like online there was a woman who was getting beat up or taken against her will.  There were men nearby that ran to help and beat up the guy.  This is a feel good fight because justice has happened.  The suspect deserved what he got. Although I don't like seeing people get killed.  No one deserves to get killed regardless.  

Boxing, I don't really care for boxing because people are intentionally hurting each other.  That's what they decided to do.  It makes me grimace if I see a boxer bleeding on the boxing ring.  I just tell myself they are grown, they know when to stop if they want to.  They're doing this to make money so that's on them.  

If I saw family fighting each other, then that would stress me out.  I would feel distressed.  That would not be good at all.  I dislike conflict between family members.  I want everyone to get along and be at peace with each other.  It feels good to not have any conflict.  Live in harmony is the best way to go.
srp_clean_factor2_criminaltendencies_12_:

When I was young, I recall having a mace on me just in case because I liked to go out in the nighttime and stay out late.  I usually was always with people so I didn't worry about it.  I do not recall a time that I felt my life was in danger.  I just watch people and try to be aware.  Driving home at night, I do recall feeling like I need to be careful and be watchful.  Keep my doors locked and be aware of my surroundings.  

At this point, I am old, I just avoid going out to places that I feel are risky.  I do not go out at night.  Will not walk outside at night.  I keep my doors and windows locked.  It's the best I can do.  I don't have a way to protect myself and I don't want to have to buy guns because I don't believe in owning guns.  Guns should not exist in the first place.

machiv_clean_interpersonal_tactics_3_bro: There have been many situations where I felt like it woouldd be the right thing to do in order to defuse the situation that was occuring, but didn't do it, because morally it was not the right thing to do, given that what they were doing was as wronng as people were claiming it was who ere arguing with them. For example, in one instance, I was working on a project that involved creating a Esports website for a client. I was wworking for someone else on the project and not leading it myself ad so they were the one's who were to make the final decisions and determine what would occur throughout the project. I knew, from interacting with them and through others stories of interacting with them, that they were ap to fire someone or chastize someone in front of others who disagreed with them, even when the person was in the right. They were insisting that we increase the size of the embeded Youtube videos, despite our protest that increasing the size of them would put undue strain on the website. They kept disagreeing with us and saying that we were wrong and that since it was hosted on Youtube that would not happen. We tried to explain to them that they were refering to the impact on bandwith to the site, but not taking into account the extra space and load time that was associated with it when our website was communicating with Youtube to stream the video. They started to become angry and agitated and I knew that we had other task to do and so I messaged the other members of the team as they were talking and told them that perhap it was best if we just went ahead and did it and then addressed it again once we were live testing the website. In this way he could see it happenn in real time and be able to compare the two diifferent versions and in this way it would be harder for him to argue against it. It was in that moment that I decided to just tell him we would do it. I didn't flatter him or tell him he was right, because he wasn't and I didn't want to set that precedent, even though he was the team lead. Upon hearing it, he calmed down and we were able to get back to work. Ultimately, when it became time to live test the website we showed him two different versions of the site. One with the large Youtube embedded videos he requested and the one with the properly sized videos that we wanted. The layout and load times for our version was much better and when we had testers come in and experience that portion of the website, they liked our version better than the one he had suggested. As a result, he ultimately decided to relent d let us go ahead with our interation of the website without complaining about it any further. It allowed us to make creative and technical decisionns without worrry and he did end up appologizing to the team later on after the site had been live and he had started to get a lot of positive feedback about how much everyone enjoyed the website.
machiv_clean_views_of_human_nature_13_sp: In this situation, I would image that someone in the group that is watching would ultimately step in and say or do something, but this doesn't always happen. I think that, given this scenario, it would really depend on the entire context of the situation annd what all transpired leading up to this situation that would allow me to better determine if the crowd of people would step in or not. For example, if the one person who was harassing the other person had already started hurting them physical in some way for a bit of time and no one stepped in to stop it at that point in time, I wouldn't expect anyone in that group of people to step in and put a stop to it, because if they were going to, they would have done it as soon as things escalated. This doesn't mean they won't call the cops or something, but they are not ap to step in themselves, due to the risk it poses to them. I do think people are willing to step in and take risk to help others in public, but it is usually measured and I often notice that it depends on how many people are willing to step in or say something in a given situation. There are times when I have see a single person step in before I was even able to do something, but this is fairly uncommon and I can certainly understand why.
